Laboratory studies reveal serum calcium of 7.2 mg/dL (normal 8.5-10.5), intact parathyroid hormone of 8 pg/mL (normal 15-65), and absolute CD4+ T-cell count of 450 cells/μL (normal 2000-4500 for age). Chest X-ray shows absent thymic shadow. Fluorescence in situ hybridization testing is ordered.

options.text: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome with partial DiGeorge phenotype
options.explanation: Correct. This patient has the classic CATCH-22 features: cardiac defects (tetralogy of Fallot), abnormal facies (low-set ears), thymic hypoplasia (absent thymic shadow), cleft palate, and hypocalcemia with hypoparathyroidism. The CD4+ count of 450 cells/μL indicates partial DiGeorge syndrome, where T-cell function is preserved but reduced.

options.text: Severe combined immunodeficiency with complete T-cell absence
options.explanation: Incorrect. Complete DiGeorge syndrome (<1% of cases) would show absent T-cells, not the reduced but present CD4+ count of 450 cells/μL seen here. This patient has functional T-cells consistent with partial DiGeorge syndrome.

options.text: CHARGE syndrome with associated immunodeficiency
options.explanation: Incorrect. While CHARGE syndrome can present with cardiac defects and cleft palate, it typically includes coloboma, heart defects, atresia choanae, retarded growth, genital abnormalities, and ear abnormalities. The combination of hypoparathyroidism and thymic hypoplasia is more specific for 22q11.2 deletion.

options.text: Velocardiofacial syndrome without immunological involvement
options.explanation: Incorrect. Velocardiofacial syndrome is part of the 22q11.2 deletion spectrum, but this patient clearly has immunological involvement evidenced by recurrent infections, reduced CD4+ T-cell count, and absent thymic shadow indicating thymic hypoplasia.

options.text: Primary hypoparathyroidism with coincidental cardiac malformation
options.explanation: Incorrect. While this patient has hypoparathyroidism, the constellation of findings including cardiac defects, cleft palate, facial dysmorphism, thymic hypoplasia, and T-cell deficiency indicates a syndromic diagnosis rather than isolated hypoparathyroidism with coincidental findings.

digeorge-22q11.explanation: This patient presents with the classic features of 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (DiGeorge syndrome): cardiac defects (tetralogy of Fallot), abnormal facies (low-set ears), thymic hypoplasia (absent thymic shadow), cleft palate, and hypocalcemia due to hypoparathyroidism - forming the CATCH-22 acronym. The key distinguishing feature is the CD4+ T-cell count of 450 cells/μL, which indicates partial DiGeorge syndrome (the most common form, ~99% of cases). In partial DiGeorge, patients have reduced but functional T-cells (typically CD4+ 200-1,500 cells/μL), allowing for some immune function. Complete DiGeorge syndrome (<1% of cases) would show absent T-cells and present as SCID-like illness. The neonatal seizures due to hypocalcemia are often the first presentation in the NICU, making this a classic case presentation.
digeorge-22q11.key_concept: T-cell range: partial DiGeorge (most) — CD4 200–1,500, functional. Complete DiGeorge (<1%) — T cells absent, SCID-like.
digeorge-22q11.reasoning: This case requires analysis of multiple complex clinical features (CATCH-22 syndrome) to diagnose partial DiGeorge syndrome/22q11.2 deletion. The learner must synthesize cardiac, immunologic, endocrine, and dysmorphic findings, distinguish between partial vs complete forms based on T-cell counts, and understand the broader spectrum of 22q11.2 deletion disorders. This represents competent-level medical knowledge requiring analytical thinking beyond simple recall.
digeorge-22q11.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases (PIDD). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/primary-immune-deficiency-diseases

digeorge-22q11.stem: A 6-month-old male infant is brought to the pediatric immunology clinic for evaluation of recurrent severe infections. He was born at 37 weeks gestation and required NICU admission for neonatal seizures due to hypocalcemia. Physical examination reveals micrognathia, low-set ears, and a harsh systolic murmur consistent with tetralogy of Fallot. Laboratory studies show undetectable CD3+ T cells (<50 cells/μL), normal B-cell count, and elevated IgM with decreased IgG. Chest CT demonstrates complete absence of thymic tissue. Chromosomal microarray confirms 22q11.2 deletion. The patient has had multiple hospitalizations for Pneumocystis pneumonia and disseminated CMV infection despite prophylactic antimicrobials and IVIG.

options.text: Cultured thymus transplantation from a deceased donor
options.explanation: Correct. Complete DiGeorge syndrome requires thymus transplantation to provide a functional environment for T-cell development and maturation.

options.text: Matched sibling hematopoietic stem cell transplantation
options.explanation: Incorrect. Without a thymus, transplanted stem cells cannot differentiate into functional T cells, making HSCT ineffective in complete DiGeorge syndrome.

options.text: Haploidentical parental bone marrow transplantation
options.explanation: Incorrect. Any form of HSCT will fail because the absent thymus cannot educate donor T cells, regardless of the donor source.

options.text: Autologous gene therapy with TBX1 replacement
options.explanation: Incorrect. While TBX1 mutations contribute to DiGeorge syndrome, gene therapy cannot regenerate thymic tissue that failed to develop embryologically.

options.text: Immunoglobulin replacement with T-cell growth factors
options.explanation: Incorrect. IVIG and cytokines cannot substitute for the structural thymic environment required for T-cell development and selection.

digeorge-22q11.explanation: This infant has complete DiGeorge syndrome, evidenced by absent thymic tissue on imaging and undetectable T cells. The definitive treatment is cultured thymus transplantation from a deceased donor, which provides the thymic microenvironment necessary for T-cell development. HSCT is ineffective because without a thymus, donor stem cells cannot mature into functional T cells. The thymus transplant will allow the patient's own stem cells to develop into educated, functional T cells.
digeorge-22q11.key_concept: Complete DiGeorge syndrome requires thymus transplantation, not HSCT, because absent thymic tissue cannot educate donor T cells
digeorge-22q11.reasoning: This complex case requires understanding the pathophysiology of complete DiGeorge syndrome, distinguishing it from other SCIDs, and knowing that thymus transplantation is uniquely required due to absent thymic tissue preventing T-cell education. The question tests advanced knowledge of rare immunodeficiency treatments and requires analysis of why standard HSCT approaches fail in this specific condition.
digeorge-22q11.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases (PIDD). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/primary-immune-deficiency-diseases

dress-syndrome.stem: A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with fever, generalized rash, and facial swelling that developed over the past 3 days. He reports starting allopurinol 6 weeks ago for gout prophylaxis after his first acute attack. Physical examination reveals temperature 38.9°C, diffuse erythematous maculopapular rash with desquamation, periorbital edema, and cervical lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies show white blood cell count 15,200/μL with 18% eosinophils, AST 156 U/L, ALT 189 U/L, and creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL). Blood cultures are negative. HHV-6 DNA is detected by PCR.

options.text: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS)
options.explanation: Correct. Classic DRESS presentation with 6-week latency after allopurinol (common culprit), fever, rash, eosinophilia, organ involvement (liver, kidney), and HHV-6 reactivation which is pathognomonic for DRESS.

options.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome with toxic epidermal necrolysis
options.explanation: Incorrect. SJS/TEN typically occurs within days of drug exposure, lacks eosinophilia and HHV-6 reactivation, and presents with mucosal involvement and skin detachment rather than this presentation.

options.text: Acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP)
options.explanation: Incorrect. AGEP presents with sterile pustules on erythematous skin, occurs within hours to days of drug exposure, and lacks the systemic organ involvement and HHV-6 reactivation seen here.

options.text: Serum sickness-like syndrome from allopurinol
options.explanation: Incorrect. Serum sickness-like syndrome typically occurs 1-3 weeks after drug initiation, lacks eosinophilia and HHV-6 reactivation, and doesn't cause significant hepatic or renal involvement.

options.text: Hypereosinophilic syndrome with secondary organ damage
options.explanation: Incorrect. While this explains the eosinophilia and organ involvement, it doesn't account for the temporal relationship with allopurinol, acute onset, or HHV-6 reactivation which is specific to DRESS.

dress-syndrome.explanation: This patient presents with DRESS syndrome, characterized by the classic triad of drug exposure (allopurinol 6 weeks prior), systemic symptoms (fever, rash, lymphadenopathy), and internal organ involvement (hepatitis, nephritis). The 6-week latency period is typical for DRESS, which has the longest onset time of any severe cutaneous adverse reaction (SCAR). The presence of eosinophilia and HHV-6 reactivation are distinguishing features that differentiate DRESS from other drug hypersensitivity reactions. The RegiSCAR criteria are met with fever, rash, hospitalization, organ involvement, and eosinophilia. Allopurinol is a well-known DRESS trigger, particularly in patients with certain HLA alleles.
dress-syndrome.key_concept: DRESS timing: 2–8 weeks after drug. Longest latency of any SCAR.
dress-syndrome.reasoning: This question tests knowledge of DRESS syndrome, a severe drug hypersensitivity reaction. It requires analysis of clinical presentation, timing, laboratory findings, and distinguishing features from other SCARs. The milestone level 3 reflects competent recognition of this serious condition that requires prompt diagnosis and management in independent practice.
dress-syndrome.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

dress-syndrome.stem: A 42-year-old man with epilepsy presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of fever, facial swelling, and a generalized maculopapular rash. He was started on phenytoin 6 weeks ago after his first seizure. Temperature is 38.9°C (102°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 140/85 mmHg. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema, cervical lymphadenopathy, and an erythematous maculopapular rash covering 80% of his body surface area. Laboratory studies show leukocyte count 15,000/μL with 12% eosinophils and 8% atypical lymphocytes, AST 180 U/L, ALT 220 U/L, and creatinine 1.8 mg/dL. Skin biopsy shows interface dermatitis with eosinophilic infiltration but no full-thickness epidermal necrosis.

options.text: Human herpesvirus-6 polymerase chain reaction testing
options.explanation: Correct. HHV-6 reactivation is a distinguishing feature of DRESS syndrome and correlates with disease severity, helping differentiate from other severe cutaneous adverse reactions.

options.text: Patch testing with phenytoin and related anticonvulsants
options.explanation: Incorrect. Patch testing is contraindicated in acute DRESS and is not useful for diagnosis during the acute phase of severe cutaneous adverse reactions.

options.text: Skin biopsy for direct immunofluorescence studies
options.explanation: Incorrect. Direct immunofluorescence is used to diagnose autoimmune bullous diseases, not drug hypersensitivity reactions like DRESS syndrome.

options.text: Serum tryptase level and complement studies
options.explanation: Incorrect. Tryptase elevation occurs in anaphylaxis and mastocytosis, while complement studies are relevant for immune complex diseases, neither of which fits this presentation.

options.text: Bacterial blood cultures and procalcitonin level
options.explanation: Incorrect. While fever and leukocytosis are present, the constellation of rash, eosinophilia, organ involvement, and drug timing strongly suggests DRESS rather than bacterial sepsis.

dress-syndrome.explanation: This patient presents with classic DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) based on the RegiSCAR criteria: fever, extensive rash, hospitalization, internal organ involvement (liver and kidney), and eosinophilia with atypical lymphocytes, occurring 6 weeks after phenytoin initiation. HHV-6 reactivation is the distinguishing feature of DRESS that differentiates it from other severe cutaneous adverse reactions like Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis, and it correlates with disease severity. The other options are either contraindicated in acute DRESS (patch testing), irrelevant to the diagnosis (DIF, tryptase, complement), or address incorrect differential diagnoses (bacterial infection).
dress-syndrome.key_concept: HHV-6 reactivation is the distinguishing diagnostic feature of DRESS syndrome that differentiates it from other severe cutaneous adverse reactions
dress-syndrome.reasoning: This question requires advanced knowledge of DRESS syndrome diagnosis, specifically the role of HHV-6 reactivation as a distinguishing feature. The case requires analysis of complex clinical presentation with multiple organ involvement and differentiation from other severe cutaneous adverse reactions. This represents proficient-level medical knowledge requiring management of a complex, potentially life-threatening drug hypersensitivity reaction.
dress-syndrome.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

dress-syndrome.stem: A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, facial swelling, and a diffuse skin eruption. He was started on phenytoin 6 weeks ago following a seizure. Temperature is 39.2°C (102.6°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 140/85 mmHg. Physical examination shows facial edema, cervical lymphadenopathy, and an erythematous maculopapular rash covering 80% of his body surface area. Laboratory studies show leukocyte count 15,000/μL with 20% eosinophils and 15% atypical lymphocytes, AST 180 U/L, ALT 220 U/L, and creatinine 1.8 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS)
options.explanation: Correct. This patient meets RegiSCAR criteria with fever, hospitalization-requiring rash, eosinophilia >1500/μL, atypical lymphocytes, and organ involvement (liver and kidney). The 6-week latency after phenytoin initiation is classic for DRESS.

options.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome with toxic epidermal necrolysis
options.explanation: Incorrect. SJS/TEN typically occurs 1-3 weeks after drug exposure, presents with mucosal involvement and skin detachment rather than facial swelling and lymphadenopathy, and lacks the eosinophilia and atypical lymphocytes seen in DRESS.

options.text: Acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP)
options.explanation: Incorrect. AGEP has a much shorter onset (1-2 days), presents with sterile pustules rather than maculopapular rash, and typically shows neutrophilia rather than eosinophilia with atypical lymphocytes.

options.text: Viral exanthem with secondary bacterial infection
options.explanation: Incorrect. Viral exanthems do not typically cause the constellation of eosinophilia, atypical lymphocytes, significant organ dysfunction, and facial swelling seen in this patient. The timing after drug initiation is also suggestive of drug reaction.

options.text: Serum sickness-like syndrome from phenytoin
options.explanation: Incorrect. While serum sickness can cause fever, rash, and lymphadenopathy, it typically lacks the marked eosinophilia, atypical lymphocytes, and significant hepatic and renal dysfunction characteristic of DRESS syndrome.

dress-syndrome.explanation: This patient presents with classic DRESS syndrome, meeting the RegiSCAR criteria: fever (39.2°C), hospitalization-requiring rash (80% BSA involvement), eosinophilia (20% = ~3000/μL), atypical lymphocytes (15%), and internal organ involvement (elevated transaminases and creatinine). The 6-week latency after phenytoin initiation is typical for DRESS, which has the longest onset among severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCARs). The combination of facial swelling, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia, and multi-organ involvement distinguishes DRESS from other drug reactions like SJS/TEN or AGEP.
dress-syndrome.key_concept: RegiSCAR ≥4: fever + rash + hospitalization + internal organ + eosinophilia ± atypical lymphocytes
dress-syndrome.reasoning: This question requires analysis of clinical presentation, laboratory findings, and timeline to differentiate DRESS syndrome from other severe cutaneous adverse reactions. The learner must synthesize multiple diagnostic criteria (RegiSCAR) and distinguish between similar drug hypersensitivity conditions, representing competent-level medical knowledge in drug allergy.
dress-syndrome.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

dress-syndrome.stem: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with fever, facial swelling, and a generalized maculopapular rash that began 4 weeks after starting phenytoin for seizure control. His temperature is 38.9°C (102.0°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema, cervical lymphadenopathy, and an erythematous rash covering 60% of his body surface area. Laboratory studies show white blood cell count 15,000/μL with 12% eosinophils, AST 180 U/L, ALT 220 U/L, and creatinine 1.8 mg/dL. Blood cultures are negative. Which organ system is most commonly affected in this patient's condition?

options.text: Hepatic involvement with transaminase elevation
options.explanation: Correct. This patient has DRESS syndrome, and hepatic involvement with transaminase elevation is the most common internal organ manifestation, occurring in approximately 75% of cases.

options.text: Renal involvement with acute interstitial nephritis
options.explanation: Incorrect. While renal involvement occurs in DRESS syndrome and is present in this patient (elevated creatinine), it affects only about 10-15% of patients and is not the most common organ involvement.

options.text: Pulmonary involvement with eosinophilic pneumonitis
options.explanation: Incorrect. Pulmonary involvement can occur in DRESS syndrome but is less common than hepatic involvement, affecting approximately 10% of patients. This patient shows no respiratory symptoms.

options.text: Cardiac involvement with eosinophilic myocarditis
options.explanation: Incorrect. Cardiac involvement is a serious but uncommon manifestation of DRESS syndrome, occurring in less than 10% of cases. Hepatic involvement is much more frequent.

options.text: Neurologic involvement with aseptic meningitis
options.explanation: Incorrect. While neurologic complications can rarely occur in DRESS syndrome, they are not a recognized common organ involvement. The most frequent internal organ affected is the liver.

dress-syndrome.explanation: This patient presents with DRESS (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) syndrome, evidenced by the classic triad of fever, rash, and internal organ involvement occurring 2-8 weeks after drug initiation (phenytoin). The RegiSCAR criteria are met with fever, hospitalization-requiring rash, eosinophilia (12%), and multi-organ involvement. Hepatic involvement with transaminase elevation is the most common internal organ manifestation of DRESS, occurring in approximately 75% of cases. While this patient also shows renal involvement (elevated creatinine), hepatic involvement remains the most frequent. Other organ systems like lungs, heart, and kidneys can be affected but with lower frequency than the liver.
dress-syndrome.key_concept: Hepatic involvement with transaminase elevation is the most common internal organ manifestation in DRESS syndrome
dress-syndrome.reasoning: This question tests knowledge of DRESS syndrome, a severe drug hypersensitivity reaction that falls under drug allergy domain. It requires analysis of clinical presentation to identify the syndrome and knowledge of which organ systems are most commonly affected. This represents competent-level medical knowledge as it involves recognizing a complex drug reaction and understanding its typical manifestations.
dress-syndrome.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

dress-syndrome.stem: A 42-year-old Han Chinese man presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of fever, facial swelling, and a diffuse maculopapular rash. He reports starting allopurinol 6 weeks ago for gout prophylaxis after his first acute gout attack. Physical examination reveals temperature 38.9°C, facial edema, cervical lymphadenopathy, and an erythematous maculopapular rash covering 40% of his body surface area. Laboratory studies show white blood cell count 14,000/μL with 18% eosinophils, ALT 245 U/L, AST 198 U/L, and creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 mg/dL). Blood cultures are negative. HHV-6 DNA is detected by PCR.

options.text: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS)
options.explanation: Correct. Classic DRESS presentation with allopurinol in Han Chinese patient, 6-week latency, fever, rash, eosinophilia, hepatitis, nephritis, and pathognomonic HHV-6 reactivation.

options.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome with toxic epidermal necrolysis
options.explanation: Incorrect. SJS/TEN typically has shorter latency (1-3 weeks), mucosal involvement, skin detachment, and lacks eosinophilia and HHV-6 reactivation seen in DRESS.

options.text: Acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP)
options.explanation: Incorrect. AGEP has rapid onset (1-2 days), sterile pustules rather than maculopapular rash, and lacks the systemic organ involvement and HHV-6 reactivation of DRESS.

options.text: Hypereosinophilic syndrome with secondary organ involvement
options.explanation: Incorrect. HES is not drug-related, lacks the typical timing and HHV-6 reactivation, and would have much higher eosinophil counts (>1,500/μL persistently).

options.text: Viral exanthem with secondary bacterial superinfection
options.explanation: Incorrect. Viral exanthems lack eosinophilia and specific organ toxicity pattern. HHV-6 detection represents reactivation in DRESS, not primary viral illness.

dress-syndrome.explanation: This patient presents with classic DRESS syndrome. Key features include: (1) allopurinol use in a Han Chinese patient (HLA-B*58:01 association), (2) characteristic 6-week latency period, (3) fever and extensive rash, (4) eosinophilia >10%, (5) multi-organ involvement (hepatitis and nephritis), and (6) pathognomonic HHV-6 reactivation. The RegiSCAR score would be ≥4, confirming the diagnosis. SJS/TEN lacks eosinophilia and HHV-6 reactivation. AGEP has rapid onset with pustules. HES is not drug-related and lacks HHV-6. Viral exanthem doesn't cause this pattern of organ toxicity.
dress-syndrome.key_concept: DRESS syndrome diagnosis with allopurinol in Han Chinese patient showing characteristic latency, eosinophilia, organ involvement, and HHV-6 reactivation
dress-syndrome.reasoning: This complex DRESS syndrome case requires advanced medical knowledge to differentiate from similar severe cutaneous adverse reactions. The question tests analysis of clinical presentation, laboratory findings, and pathognomonic features (HHV-6 reactivation, ethnic predisposition) requiring proficient-level understanding of drug hypersensitivity mechanisms and diagnostic criteria.
dress-syndrome.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.stem: A 45-year-old man with MRSA bacteremia is receiving intravenous vancomycin 1.5 g over 30 minutes. Twenty minutes into the infusion, he develops intense flushing and pruritus involving his face, neck, and upper chest. His vital signs show blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, heart rate 95 bpm, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. He has no respiratory distress or angioedema. The infusion is paused and his symptoms resolve within 15 minutes. He has no known drug allergies and has never received vancomycin previously.

options.text: Stop the infusion permanently and administer diphenhydramine, then switch to linezolid
options.explanation: Incorrect. Red man syndrome is not a contraindication to vancomycin use and does not require permanent discontinuation.

options.text: Restart the infusion at the same rate after administering epinephrine intramuscularly
options.explanation: Incorrect. Epinephrine is not indicated for red man syndrome, which is not an IgE-mediated anaphylactic reaction.

options.text: Administer diphenhydramine and restart the infusion over 90 minutes
options.explanation: Correct. This represents red man syndrome caused by direct mast cell activation. Treatment involves slowing the infusion rate and antihistamines.

options.text: Order vancomycin-specific IgE testing before any future vancomycin administration
options.explanation: Incorrect. Red man syndrome is not IgE-mediated, so specific IgE testing would not be useful for this non-allergic reaction.

options.text: Document a vancomycin allergy and use only oral antibiotics going forward
options.explanation: Incorrect. Red man syndrome is not a true drug allergy and should not be documented as such, as this limits important treatment options.

drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.explanation: This patient is experiencing vancomycin red man syndrome (RMS), a non-allergic infusion reaction caused by direct activation of mast cells via MRGPRX2 receptors, leading to histamine release. The classic presentation includes flushing, pruritus, and erythema involving the face, neck, and upper torso - areas with high mast cell density. RMS is rate-dependent and typically occurs with rapid infusions. Management involves pausing the infusion, administering antihistamines like diphenhydramine, and restarting at a slower rate (≥60 minutes for doses ≤1g, ≥90 minutes for doses >1g). This is not an IgE-mediated allergic reaction, so epinephrine is not indicated, IgE testing is not useful, and vancomycin can be continued safely with appropriate precautions.
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.key_concept: Red man syndrome is a non-allergic, rate-dependent mast cell activation reaction to vancomycin managed by slowing infusion rate and antihistamines
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.reasoning: This question tests understanding of vancomycin red man syndrome, a classic non-IgE mediated drug reaction. It requires analyzing clinical presentation to distinguish between allergic and non-allergic reactions, understanding the mechanism of direct mast cell activation, and applying appropriate management strategies. The competent level is appropriate as this represents independent management of a common adverse drug reaction scenario.
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.stem: A 28-year-old man with methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus osteomyelitis is receiving intravenous vancomycin 1 gram over 30 minutes. Twenty minutes into the infusion, he develops intense flushing and pruritus involving his face, neck, and upper chest. His vital signs show blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, heart rate 95/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. The nurse stops the infusion and administers diphenhydramine 50 mg intravenously. The patient's symptoms resolve completely within 45 minutes. Which anatomical distribution pattern best explains this patient's reaction?

options.text: Higher concentration of mast cells in the face, neck, and upper torso
options.explanation: Correct. Red man syndrome affects areas with high mast cell density, explaining the characteristic distribution pattern of flushing in the face, neck, and upper chest.

options.text: Increased vascular permeability in gravity-dependent regions
options.explanation: Incorrect. Gravity-dependent distribution would affect lower extremities first, not the face and neck which are the primary areas affected in red man syndrome.

options.text: Greater sympathetic innervation of facial and cervical blood vessels
options.explanation: Incorrect. Red man syndrome is caused by direct mast cell activation and histamine release, not sympathetic nervous system involvement.

options.text: Enhanced drug penetration through thinner skin of the head and neck
options.explanation: Incorrect. The reaction is not due to drug penetration through skin but rather systemic mast cell activation with preferential distribution based on mast cell density.

options.text: Preferential binding of vancomycin to receptors in upper body tissues
options.explanation: Incorrect. While vancomycin does activate MRGPRX2 receptors on mast cells, the distribution pattern is determined by mast cell density, not receptor distribution.

drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.explanation: Red man syndrome is caused by vancomycin's direct activation of MRGPRX2 receptors on mast cells, leading to histamine release. The characteristic distribution involving the face, neck, and upper torso occurs because these anatomical regions have the highest concentration of mast cells. This explains why the flushing and erythema predominantly affect these areas rather than being generalized. The rapid resolution with antihistamines and the ability to restart vancomycin at a slower infusion rate confirm this is not an IgE-mediated allergic reaction but rather a predictable, dose-rate dependent phenomenon.
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.key_concept: Red man syndrome distribution correlates with anatomical areas of high mast cell density
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.reasoning: This question tests understanding of red man syndrome pathophysiology and anatomical distribution patterns. It requires analyzing the mechanism (mast cell activation via MRGPRX2) and correlating it with anatomical mast cell density to explain the characteristic distribution. This represents competent-level medical knowledge requiring analytical thinking to connect pathophysiology with clinical presentation.
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.stem: A 45-year-old man with MRSA bacteremia is receiving his second dose of vancomycin 1.5g IV over 30 minutes. Twenty minutes into the infusion, he develops flushing of his face and neck, pruritus, and erythema extending to his upper chest. His vital signs are stable with blood pressure 128/82 mmHg, heart rate 88/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. He has no shortness of breath, wheezing, or urticaria elsewhere. His previous vancomycin dose was administered over 45 minutes without incident.

options.text: Stop the infusion permanently and administer diphenhydramine 50 mg IV
options.explanation: Incorrect. While diphenhydramine is appropriate, permanently stopping vancomycin is unnecessary as this represents red man syndrome, not a true allergy.

options.text: Continue the infusion at the same rate and monitor closely
options.explanation: Incorrect. The infusion should be paused to prevent worsening symptoms, as red man syndrome is dose-rate dependent.

options.text: Pause the infusion, administer diphenhydramine 25 mg IV, then restart at a slower rate
options.explanation: Correct. This is the standard management for red man syndrome: pause infusion, give antihistamine, then restart more slowly.

options.text: Stop the infusion and switch to linezolid for MRSA coverage
options.explanation: Incorrect. Red man syndrome is not a contraindication to vancomycin use and can be prevented with slower infusion rates.

options.text: Administer epinephrine 0.3 mg IM and call for anaphylaxis protocol
options.explanation: Incorrect. This presentation lacks signs of anaphylaxis (no hypotension, bronchospasm, or widespread urticaria) and represents red man syndrome.

drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.explanation: This patient is experiencing red man syndrome (RMS), a non-IgE mediated reaction caused by direct mast cell activation via MRGPRX2 receptors, leading to histamine release. The classic presentation includes flushing, pruritus, and erythema of the face, neck, and upper torso due to high mast cell density in these areas. RMS is rate-dependent and occurs with rapid vancomycin infusion. Management involves pausing the infusion, administering diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV, and restarting at a slower rate (≥60 minutes for doses ≤1g, ≥90 minutes for >1g). This is NOT a contraindication to vancomycin use, unlike true allergic reactions.
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.key_concept: Red man syndrome management requires pausing infusion, antihistamine administration, and slower re-infusion rather than permanent vancomycin discontinuation
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.reasoning: This question tests the ability to distinguish red man syndrome from true drug allergy and apply appropriate management. It requires understanding of non-IgE mediated reactions, MRGPRX2 receptor pathways, and rate-dependent vancomycin reactions. The clinical scenario requires independent decision-making for a common drug reaction, representing competent-level patient care skills with practical application of medical knowledge.
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.stem: A 45-year-old man with methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia is receiving vancomycin 1.5 g intravenously. Fifteen minutes into the infusion, he develops facial flushing, neck erythema, and pruritus involving his upper chest. His blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, heart rate is 88/min, and he denies shortness of breath or throat tightness. The nurse stops the infusion and administers diphenhydramine 50 mg intravenously. His symptoms resolve within 30 minutes. The patient requires continued vancomycin therapy for his infection.

options.text: Resume vancomycin at a slower infusion rate with antihistamine premedication
options.explanation: This is correct. Red man syndrome is caused by direct mast cell degranulation, not IgE-mediated allergy. The patient can safely receive vancomycin with slower infusion (≥90 minutes for doses >1g) and H1 antihistamine premedication.

options.text: Discontinue vancomycin permanently and switch to linezolid
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Red man syndrome is not an allergic reaction and is not a contraindication to vancomycin use. The patient can safely receive vancomycin with appropriate precautions.

options.text: Perform skin testing before resuming vancomycin therapy
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Red man syndrome involves direct mast cell activation via MRGPRX2 receptors, not IgE-mediated hypersensitivity. Skin testing is not indicated or useful for this non-allergic reaction.

options.text: Resume vancomycin at the same infusion rate with corticosteroid premedication
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The infusion rate must be slowed to prevent recurrence. Additionally, H1 antihistamines, not corticosteroids, are the appropriate premedication for red man syndrome prevention.

options.text: Obtain serum tryptase levels before resuming vancomycin
options.explanation: This is incorrect. While tryptase may be elevated during red man syndrome due to mast cell degranulation, checking levels is not necessary for diagnosis or management of this well-characterized reaction.

drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.explanation: Red man syndrome is a non-allergic, rate-dependent infusion reaction caused by direct mast cell degranulation through MRGPRX2 receptors, not IgE-mediated hypersensitivity. The characteristic distribution (face, neck, upper torso) reflects areas of high mast cell density. Management includes slowing or stopping the infusion and administering antihistamines. Prevention involves infusing vancomycin over ≥60 minutes (≥90 minutes for doses >1g) and premedication with H1 antihistamines. This reaction is not a contraindication to vancomycin use.
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.key_concept: Red man syndrome prevention requires slow vancomycin infusion (≥90 min for >1g doses) and H1 antihistamine premedication
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.reasoning: This question tests understanding of red man syndrome, a non-allergic drug reaction to vancomycin. It requires differentiating between IgE-mediated allergic reactions and direct mast cell degranulation, analyzing clinical presentation, and applying knowledge of appropriate management strategies. The complexity of distinguishing mechanism and selecting proper prevention measures indicates competent-level clinical reasoning.
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.stem: A 45-year-old man with MRSA pneumonia receives his first dose of vancomycin 1500 mg infused over 30 minutes. Twenty minutes into the infusion, he develops facial flushing, neck erythema, and intense pruritus without fever. Vital signs show blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, heart rate 88 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Lung examination reveals clear breath sounds bilaterally. The infusion is stopped and symptoms resolve within 30 minutes. The patient has no known drug allergies and has never received vancomycin previously.

options.text: Discontinue vancomycin permanently and obtain allergy consultation
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Red man syndrome is not an IgE-mediated allergy and does not contraindicate future vancomycin use. Permanent discontinuation would be inappropriate for this non-allergic reaction.

options.text: Administer epinephrine 0.3 mg intramuscularly and restart vancomycin at the same rate
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The patient shows no signs of anaphylaxis (no hypotension or bronchospasm), so epinephrine is not indicated. Additionally, restarting at the same infusion rate would likely reproduce the reaction.

options.text: Give diphenhydramine 50 mg IV and restart vancomycin infusion over 90 minutes
options.explanation: This is correct. The patient has red man syndrome, a non-IgE mediated reaction caused by direct mast cell degranulation. Treatment involves antihistamines and slower infusion rates (≥60 minutes, preferably ≥90 minutes for doses >1g).

options.text: Switch to linezolid and document vancomycin allergy in the medical record
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Red man syndrome is not a true allergy and should not be documented as such. Switching antibiotics unnecessarily could lead to suboptimal treatment and antimicrobial resistance.

options.text: Obtain serum tryptase level and skin testing for vancomycin hypersensitivity
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Tryptase and skin testing are used to evaluate IgE-mediated reactions. Red man syndrome is a predictable, dose-dependent, non-immunologic reaction that doesn't require allergy testing.

drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.explanation: This patient has red man syndrome (RMS), a common non-IgE mediated reaction to vancomycin caused by direct activation of MRGPRX2 receptors on mast cells, leading to histamine release. The classic presentation includes flushing, erythema, and pruritus involving the face, neck, and upper torso - areas with high mast cell density. The absence of hypotension, bronchospasm, or respiratory distress distinguishes this from true anaphylaxis. Management involves stopping the infusion, administering H1 antihistamines (diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV), and restarting at a slower rate (≥60 minutes, ≥90 minutes for doses >1g). RMS is not a contraindication to vancomycin use and should not be documented as an allergy. Prevention involves proper infusion rates and pre-medication with antihistamines in patients with prior RMS.
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.key_concept: Red man syndrome is a non-IgE mediated reaction managed with antihistamines and slower infusion rates, not epinephrine or vancomycin discontinuation
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.reasoning: This question requires analyzing a clinical scenario to differentiate red man syndrome from true IgE-mediated drug allergy, understanding the pathophysiology of non-immunologic mast cell activation, and applying appropriate management strategies. The competent-level trainee should recognize this common vancomycin reaction and manage it appropriately without unnecessary allergy consultations or drug discontinuation.
drug-allergy-vancomycin-rms.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

eczema-herpeticum.stem: A 7-year-old boy with a history of moderate atopic dermatitis presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever and worsening skin lesions. His mother reports that his usual eczematous patches on his arms and face became painful and developed multiple small blisters that then turned into shallow, punched-out sores. Physical examination reveals temperature 38.9°C (102°F), bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, and numerous 2-4 mm umbilicated erosions clustered over inflamed eczematous skin on his forearms and cheeks. The lesions have a characteristic punched-out appearance with surrounding erythema. A Tzanck smear from one of the lesions shows multinucleated giant cells.

options.text: Eczema herpeticum requiring immediate intravenous acyclovir therapy
options.explanation: Correct. This presentation of punched-out umbilicated erosions in eczematous skin with fever and lymphadenopathy, plus Tzanck smear showing multinucleated giant cells, is classic for eczema herpeticum. This is a dermatologic emergency requiring IV acyclovir.

options.text: Impetigo with secondary bacterial superinfection requiring topical mupirocin
options.explanation: Incorrect. Impetigo typically presents with honey-crusted lesions, not the characteristic punched-out umbilicated erosions. The Tzanck smear showing multinucleated giant cells indicates viral, not bacterial infection.

options.text: Eczema vaccinatum requiring vaccinia immune globulin administration
options.explanation: Incorrect. Eczema vaccinatum occurs after smallpox vaccination and presents with similar lesions, but this patient has no vaccination history. The Tzanck smear findings are consistent with HSV, not vaccinia virus.

options.text: Contact dermatitis with secondary bacterial infection requiring oral antibiotics
options.explanation: Incorrect. Contact dermatitis would not present with the characteristic punched-out umbilicated erosions, fever, or lymphadenopathy. The Tzanck smear findings indicate viral infection, not bacterial.

options.text: Molluscum contagiosum with inflammatory response requiring topical corticosteroids
options.explanation: Incorrect. While molluscum can present with umbilicated lesions, they are typically painless, pearl-like papules without the acute presentation of fever and lymphadenopathy. Tzanck smear would not show multinucleated giant cells in molluscum.

eczema-herpeticum.explanation: This patient has eczema herpeticum, a serious complication of atopic dermatitis where HSV disseminates through compromised skin. The deficient LL-37 (cathelicidin) antimicrobial peptide in atopic dermatitis patients impairs antiviral defense, predisposing to this condition. The classic triad of punched-out umbilicated erosions in eczematous skin, fever, and lymphadenopathy, combined with multinucleated giant cells on Tzanck smear, confirms the diagnosis. This is a dermatologic emergency requiring immediate IV acyclovir therapy. The other options represent different skin conditions that would not present with this constellation of findings or require different treatments.
eczema-herpeticum.key_concept: Eczema herpeticum: HSV dissemination in atopic dermatitis due to deficient LL-37 antimicrobial peptide
eczema-herpeticum.reasoning: This complex case requires recognizing eczema herpeticum as a dermatologic emergency in atopic dermatitis, analyzing clinical findings (punched-out lesions, fever, Tzanck smear results), understanding pathophysiology (deficient LL-37 antimicrobial peptide), and differentiating from other skin conditions. This represents proficient-level knowledge for managing complex atopic dermatitis complications.
eczema-herpeticum.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/eczema

eczema-herpeticum.stem: A 7-year-old boy with a history of moderate atopic dermatitis presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of worsening skin lesions and fever. His mother reports that his usual eczematous patches on his face and arms suddenly developed new vesicles that quickly evolved into painful, punched-out erosions with central umbilication. Physical examination reveals temperature 38.9°C (102°F), bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, and multiple 2-4 mm erosions with hemorrhagic crusts overlying areas of chronic eczematous dermatitis on the face, neck, and upper extremities. The lesions appear monomorphic and are surrounded by erythematous halos. The patient appears ill and is refusing oral intake due to similar lesions around his mouth.

options.text: Intravenous acyclovir therapy
options.explanation: Correct. This presentation of punched-out umbilicated erosions in eczematous skin with fever and lymphadenopathy is classic for eczema herpeticum, requiring immediate IV acyclovir for severe cases.

options.text: Topical mupirocin and oral cephalexin
options.explanation: Incorrect. While bacterial superinfection can occur in atopic dermatitis, the monomorphic punched-out erosions with umbilication are not consistent with impetigo, which typically presents with honey-crusted lesions.

options.text: High-potency topical corticosteroids
options.explanation: Incorrect. Topical steroids are contraindicated in viral infections and could worsen eczema herpeticum by further suppressing local immune responses in already compromised atopic skin.

options.text: Oral prednisone and antihistamines
options.explanation: Incorrect. Systemic corticosteroids would be dangerous in this setting as they could promote viral dissemination and worsen the HSV infection in this immunocompromised skin barrier.

options.text: Topical calcineurin inhibitors
options.explanation: Incorrect. While calcineurin inhibitors are useful for atopic dermatitis maintenance, they have immunosuppressive effects and are contraindicated during active viral infections like eczema herpeticum.

eczema-herpeticum.explanation: This patient presents with classic eczema herpeticum - HSV dissemination in atopic dermatitis characterized by punched-out umbilicated erosions overlying eczematous skin, fever, and lymphadenopathy. The monomorphic appearance and rapid progression distinguish this from bacterial superinfection. IV acyclovir is indicated for severe cases (fever, extensive involvement, systemic symptoms). The deficient LL-37 cathelicidin in AD patients impairs antiviral defense, making them susceptible to HSV dissemination. Immunosuppressive treatments (steroids, calcineurin inhibitors) are contraindicated as they could worsen viral spread.
eczema-herpeticum.key_concept: Clinical recognition and acute management of eczema herpeticum with punched-out umbilicated erosions in eczematous skin plus systemic symptoms
eczema-herpeticum.reasoning: This question tests recognition and management of eczema herpeticum, a serious complication of atopic dermatitis. It requires analysis of clinical presentation (punched-out umbilicated lesions, fever, systemic symptoms) to differentiate from bacterial superinfection and select appropriate antiviral therapy while avoiding contraindicated immunosuppressive treatments. This represents competent-level patient care for managing complications of allergic skin disease.
eczema-herpeticum.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/eczema

eczema-herpeticum.stem: A 16-year-old boy with a history of moderate atopic dermatitis presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of worsening skin lesions and fever. His mother reports that his usual eczematous patches on his arms and neck have developed new painful, punched-out erosions with central umbilication. Physical examination reveals temperature of 38.9°C (102°F), bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, and multiple 2-4 mm vesicles and erosions with hemorrhagic crusts overlying areas of chronic dermatitis on his arms, neck, and face. Several lesions appear umbilicated with necrotic centers. He appears ill and reports significant pain rather than his usual pruritus. His mother mentions he had a cold sore 2 weeks ago that resolved.

options.text: Perform HSV PCR testing of vesicle fluid
options.explanation: Correct. HSV PCR is the most sensitive and specific test for diagnosing eczema herpeticum, which is highly suspected given the clinical presentation of umbilicated erosions in eczematous skin with fever and lymphadenopathy.

options.text: Obtain bacterial culture and sensitivity from lesions
options.explanation: Incorrect. While secondary bacterial infection can occur, the umbilicated vesicles with necrotic centers and systemic symptoms are classic for eczema herpeticum, not primary bacterial impetiginization.

options.text: Start topical corticosteroids for eczema flare
options.explanation: Incorrect. Topical corticosteroids are contraindicated in eczema herpeticum as they can worsen viral dissemination and delay healing.

options.text: Perform patch testing for contact allergens
options.explanation: Incorrect. The acute presentation with fever, umbilicated lesions, and systemic symptoms suggests infectious etiology rather than allergic contact dermatitis superimposed on atopic dermatitis.

options.text: Order serum IgE levels and specific allergen testing
options.explanation: Incorrect. While elevated IgE is common in atopic dermatitis, this acute presentation with characteristic morphology requires immediate evaluation for eczema herpeticum, not allergy assessment.

eczema-herpeticum.explanation: This patient presents with classic eczema herpeticum - HSV dissemination in atopic dermatitis skin. The key features are punched-out, umbilicated erosions developing in areas of pre-existing eczema, fever, and lymphadenopathy. HSV PCR is the most sensitive diagnostic test. Patients with atopic dermatitis have deficient cathelicidin (LL-37) production, leading to impaired antiviral defense and increased susceptibility to HSV dissemination. Bacterial culture would miss the viral etiology, topical steroids are contraindicated as they worsen viral spread, and allergy testing is not relevant to this acute infectious presentation.
eczema-herpeticum.key_concept: HSV PCR is the most sensitive diagnostic test for eczema herpeticum in patients with atopic dermatitis presenting with umbilicated erosions and systemic symptoms
eczema-herpeticum.reasoning: This question requires analysis of clinical presentation to diagnose eczema herpeticum in atopic dermatitis and select appropriate diagnostic testing. It falls under skin allergy domain as it involves complications of atopic dermatitis. The milestone level is 3 as it requires independent recognition of this serious complication and knowing the most appropriate diagnostic approach. Bloom's level is analysis as the learner must synthesize clinical findings to reach the correct diagnosis and testing strategy.
eczema-herpeticum.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/eczema

eczema-herpeticum.stem: A 7-year-old boy with a history of moderate atopic dermatitis presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of worsening skin lesions and fever. His mother reports that his usual eczematous patches on his arms and face began developing small, fluid-filled blisters 5 days ago, which then became painful, punched-out sores. Physical examination reveals temperature of 38.9°C (102°F), bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, and multiple 2-4 mm umbilicated erosions with crusting overlying areas of chronic eczematous dermatitis on his face, neck, and arms. The lesions appear monomorphic and clustered. A Tzanck smear from one of the lesions shows multinucleated giant cells.

options.text: Intravenous acyclovir for 10-14 days
options.explanation: Correct. This patient has eczema herpeticum with systemic symptoms (fever, lymphadenopathy) requiring IV acyclovir for 10-14 days minimum.

options.text: Oral valacyclovir for 5-7 days
options.explanation: Incorrect. While valacyclovir is appropriate for mild eczema herpeticum, this patient has severe disease with systemic symptoms requiring IV therapy and longer duration.

options.text: Topical mupirocin ointment twice daily
options.explanation: Incorrect. This treats bacterial superinfection, not the viral etiology of eczema herpeticum. The punched-out umbilicated lesions and positive Tzanck smear indicate HSV.

options.text: Oral prednisone 1 mg/kg daily for 7 days
options.explanation: Incorrect. Systemic corticosteroids are contraindicated in eczema herpeticum as they can worsen viral dissemination and delay healing.

options.text: Topical acyclovir cream five times daily
options.explanation: Incorrect. Topical antivirals have poor penetration and are inadequate for eczema herpeticum, especially with systemic involvement requiring systemic antiviral therapy.

eczema-herpeticum.explanation: This patient presents with classic eczema herpeticum, characterized by punched-out umbilicated erosions developing in areas of pre-existing atopic dermatitis, accompanied by fever and lymphadenopathy indicating systemic involvement. The positive Tzanck smear confirms HSV infection. Severe eczema herpeticum with systemic symptoms requires intravenous acyclovir for a minimum of 10-14 days. Oral valacyclovir would only be appropriate for mild cases without systemic involvement. Topical treatments are inadequate for this disseminated viral infection, and corticosteroids are contraindicated as they can worsen viral spread.
eczema-herpeticum.key_concept: Treatment: acyclovir IV for severe. Oral valacyclovir for mild. Duration: 10–14 days minimum.
eczema-herpeticum.reasoning: This question requires recognition of eczema herpeticum complicating atopic dermatitis and selection of appropriate antiviral therapy based on severity. It tests synthesis of clinical presentation (fever, lymphadenopathy, characteristic lesions, positive Tzanck smear) with treatment decision-making. The competent level is appropriate as this represents independent management of a serious complication of atopic dermatitis that allergists must recognize and treat appropriately.
eczema-herpeticum.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/eczema

eczema-herpeticum.stem: A 7-year-old boy with moderate atopic dermatitis presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of worsening skin lesions and fever. His mother reports he has been using triamcinolone 0.1% cream twice daily for the past week for an eczema flare. Physical examination reveals temperature 38.9°C, multiple punched-out vesicles and shallow erosions with central umbilication scattered throughout areas of eczematous skin on his face and arms. Several lesions appear to be coalescing. Cervical lymphadenopathy is present. Tzanck smear demonstrates multinucleated giant cells. The patient is started on intravenous acyclovir. What is the most appropriate management of his topical corticosteroid therapy?

options.text: Continue triamcinolone 0.1% cream twice daily throughout antiviral treatment
options.explanation: Incorrect. Continuing topical steroids during active eczema herpeticum can worsen viral replication and delay healing by further suppressing local immune responses.

options.text: Discontinue triamcinolone and resume after completion of antiviral therapy
options.explanation: Correct. Topical corticosteroids should be held during active eczema herpeticum as they can impair antiviral immune responses and worsen HSV dissemination. They can be safely resumed after the infection has resolved.

options.text: Switch to hydrocortisone 1% cream to reduce immunosuppressive effects
options.explanation: Incorrect. Even lower-potency topical steroids like hydrocortisone should be avoided during active eczema herpeticum, as any degree of local immunosuppression can facilitate viral replication.

options.text: Increase triamcinolone frequency to three times daily for anti-inflammatory effect
options.explanation: Incorrect. Increasing steroid frequency during active HSV infection would further compromise local antiviral immunity and potentially lead to more severe viral dissemination.

options.text: Apply triamcinolone only to unaffected eczematous areas while avoiding lesions
options.explanation: Incorrect. Even application to clinically unaffected areas poses risk during active eczema herpeticum, as HSV can spread through eczematous skin and steroids impair systemic antiviral responses.

eczema-herpeticum.explanation: This patient has eczema herpeticum (EH), characterized by punched-out umbilicated lesions in eczematous skin, fever, lymphadenopathy, and positive Tzanck smear. EH occurs due to HSV dissemination in atopic dermatitis patients who have deficient antimicrobial peptides like LL-37. The key management principle is to discontinue all topical corticosteroids during active infection, as they suppress local immune responses and can worsen viral replication and dissemination. Topical steroids can be safely resumed after the infection has completely resolved with antiviral therapy. The other options are incorrect because any continued use of topical steroids during active EH, regardless of potency, location, or frequency, can impair antiviral immunity.
eczema-herpeticum.key_concept: Hold topical steroids during active eczema herpeticum and resume after resolution
eczema-herpeticum.reasoning: This question tests knowledge of eczema herpeticum management in atopic dermatitis patients, specifically the critical decision to discontinue topical corticosteroids during active HSV infection. It requires analysis of the risk-benefit of continuing immunosuppressive therapy during viral infection and understanding of how topical steroids can impair local antiviral immunity. This represents competent-level knowledge needed for independent practice in managing complicated atopic dermatitis cases.
eczema-herpeticum.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/eczema

eoe-adult.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of progressive dysphagia to solids and two episodes of food impaction requiring emergency endoscopic removal. He denies heartburn or regurgitation. Physical examination is unremarkable. Upper endoscopy reveals longitudinal furrows, concentric rings, and white exudates throughout the esophagus. Esophageal biopsies show 22 eosinophils per high-power field with eosinophilic microabscesses. He is started on omeprazole 40 mg twice daily. Repeat endoscopy and biopsies are performed 8 weeks later, showing persistent longitudinal furrows and 20 eosinophils per high-power field.

options.text: Eosinophilic esophagitis confirmed; initiate swallowed budesonide
options.explanation: Correct. Patient meets EoE criteria: >15 eos/hpf persisting after 8-week PPI trial, ruling out PPI-responsive esophageal eosinophilia. Swallowed topical steroids are first-line therapy.

options.text: PPI-responsive esophageal eosinophilia; continue omeprazole therapy
options.explanation: Incorrect. PPI-responsive eosinophilia requires normalization of eosinophil count on PPI. This patient has persistent elevation (20 eos/hpf), indicating true EoE.

options.text: Gastroesophageal reflux disease; increase omeprazole to 80 mg daily
options.explanation: Incorrect. GERD typically doesn't cause >15 eos/hpf or characteristic endoscopic findings (rings, furrows). The persistent eosinophilia after PPI trial confirms EoE diagnosis.

options.text: Inconclusive results; extend PPI trial to 12 weeks before diagnosis
options.explanation: Incorrect. Eight weeks of PPI therapy is the standard diagnostic duration. Extending beyond 8 weeks is not required for EoE diagnosis when eosinophilia persists.

options.text: Allergic esophagitis; obtain comprehensive food allergy panel
options.explanation: Incorrect. While food allergies contribute to EoE pathogenesis, the diagnosis is established by histologic criteria after PPI trial. Allergy testing guides treatment but doesn't change the diagnosis.

eoe-adult.explanation: This patient has eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE). The diagnostic criteria include: (1) symptoms of esophageal dysfunction (dysphagia, food impaction), (2) ≥15 eosinophils per high-power field on esophageal biopsy, (3) eosinophilia persisting after 8-week proton pump inhibitor trial, and (4) exclusion of other causes. The patient's persistent eosinophil count of 20/hpf after 8 weeks of omeprazole rules out PPI-responsive esophageal eosinophilia. The endoscopic findings (longitudinal furrows, concentric rings, white exudates) are characteristic of EoE. First-line treatment includes swallowed topical corticosteroids (budesonide or fluticasone) with 50-70% efficacy, or dietary elimination therapy with 70-80% efficacy.
eoe-adult.key_concept: EoE diagnosis requires ≥15 eos/hpf persisting after 8-week PPI trial to exclude PPI-responsive eosinophilia
eoe-adult.reasoning: This question tests knowledge of eosinophilic esophagitis diagnosis and management, requiring analysis of clinical presentation, endoscopic findings, histology, and response to PPI trial. The learner must differentiate EoE from PPI-responsive eosinophilia and GERD, demonstrating competent-level clinical reasoning for this common eosinophilic GI disorder.
eoe-adult.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/eosinophilic-esophagitis

eoe-adult.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of progressive dysphagia to solids and two episodes of food impaction requiring emergency endoscopic removal. He reports no heartburn or regurgitation. Physical examination is unremarkable. Upper endoscopy reveals multiple concentric rings throughout the esophagus giving a corrugated appearance, longitudinal furrows, and white plaques on the esophageal mucosa. Esophageal biopsies show 22 eosinophils per high-power field. He was treated with high-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy for 8 weeks prior to biopsy without symptomatic improvement.

options.text: Eosinophilic esophagitis confirmed by endoscopic findings and histology
options.explanation: Correct. The patient has classic EoE presentation (dysphagia, food impaction in young male), characteristic endoscopic findings (rings/trachealization, furrows, white exudates), and diagnostic histology (≥15 eos/hpf after 8-week PPI trial).

options.text: Proton pump inhibitor-responsive esophageal eosinophilia requiring continued PPI therapy
options.explanation: Incorrect. The patient did not improve symptomatically on 8-week PPI trial, ruling out PPI-responsive eosinophilia. This diagnosis requires normalization of eosinophils and symptoms on PPI.

options.text: Gastroesophageal reflux disease with secondary esophageal eosinophilia
options.explanation: Incorrect. While GERD can cause esophageal eosinophilia, the characteristic endoscopic findings (rings, furrows, exudates) and lack of heartburn/regurgitation make this unlikely. The PPI trial also failed.

options.text: Achalasia with incidental esophageal eosinophilia requiring pneumatic dilation
options.explanation: Incorrect. Achalasia typically presents with dysphagia to both solids and liquids, not the solid-predominant pattern seen here. The endoscopic findings are not consistent with achalasia.

options.text: Candida esophagitis requiring antifungal therapy based on white plaques
options.explanation: Incorrect. While white plaques can occur in candida esophagitis, the patient is immunocompetent with no risk factors. The rings and furrows are pathognomonic for EoE, not candida.

eoe-adult.explanation: This patient has eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) based on the classic triad: 1) Clinical symptoms (dysphagia, food impaction), 2) Histologic findings (≥15 eosinophils/hpf after PPI trial), and 3) Characteristic endoscopic findings including rings (trachealization), longitudinal furrows, and white exudates. The 8-week PPI trial without improvement rules out PPI-responsive esophageal eosinophilia. The endoscopic findings described are pathognomonic for EoE and would contribute to an EREFS score used in clinical trials.
eoe-adult.key_concept: Eosinophilic esophagitis diagnosis requires characteristic endoscopic findings (rings, furrows, exudates), ≥15 eosinophils/hpf after PPI trial, and typical symptoms
eoe-adult.reasoning: This question requires analyzing clinical presentation, endoscopic findings, and histology to diagnose eosinophilic esophagitis. It tests competent-level knowledge of EoE diagnostic criteria including the requirement for PPI trial failure, characteristic endoscopic features, and eosinophil thresholds. The analysis of multiple data points (symptoms, endoscopy, histology, PPI response) to reach a diagnosis represents analysis-level cognitive work.
eoe-adult.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/eosinophilic-esophagitis

eoe-adult.stem: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe chest pain and inability to swallow after eating steak at dinner. He reports a 2-year history of progressive difficulty swallowing solid foods, requiring frequent sips of water during meals. He denies heartburn but mentions occasional food 'getting stuck' that resolves with repeated swallowing. Physical examination reveals mild distress but is otherwise normal. Chest radiograph shows no acute abnormalities. Upper endoscopy reveals a food bolus impaction in the mid-esophagus, which is successfully removed. The esophageal mucosa shows circumferential rings, longitudinal furrows, and white exudates. Esophageal biopsies demonstrate 22 eosinophils per high-power field. The patient completes an 8-week trial of high-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy, and repeat endoscopy with biopsy shows persistent findings of 20 eosinophils per high-power field.

options.text: Eosinophilic esophagitis requiring dietary elimination therapy
options.explanation: Correct. This patient meets diagnostic criteria for EoE with >15 eos/hpf persisting after adequate PPI trial, classic adult presentation of dysphagia with food impaction, and typical endoscopic findings.

options.text: Proton pump inhibitor-responsive esophageal eosinophilia
options.explanation: Incorrect. PPI-responsive eosinophilia would show normalization of eosinophil counts after PPI trial. This patient's eosinophilia persisted despite 8 weeks of PPI therapy.

options.text: Gastroesophageal reflux disease with secondary eosinophilia
options.explanation: Incorrect. While GERD can cause esophageal eosinophilia, the persistence of >15 eos/hpf after adequate PPI trial and classic EoE endoscopic findings indicate primary EoE rather than GERD.

options.text: Achalasia with concurrent allergic esophageal inflammation
options.explanation: Incorrect. Achalasia typically presents with dysphagia to both solids and liquids from onset, and endoscopy would show a tight gastroesophageal junction rather than the rings and furrows seen in EoE.

options.text: Esophageal stricture secondary to caustic injury requiring dilation
options.explanation: Incorrect. Caustic injury would have an acute onset with a clear exposure history. The chronic progressive dysphagia, specific endoscopic findings, and tissue eosinophilia are consistent with EoE, not caustic injury.

eoe-adult.explanation: This patient has eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE). The diagnosis requires ≥15 eosinophils per high-power field on esophageal biopsy that persists after an 8-week proton pump inhibitor trial. This patient demonstrates the classic adult presentation of EoE with dysphagia and food impaction, along with typical endoscopic findings including rings (trachealization), longitudinal furrows, and white exudates. The persistence of 20 eos/hpf after adequate PPI therapy confirms the diagnosis. PPI-responsive eosinophilia would normalize on PPI therapy and is not considered true EoE. The other options fail to account for the specific combination of clinical presentation, endoscopic findings, and histologic criteria that define EoE.
eoe-adult.key_concept: EoE diagnosis requires ≥15 eos/hpf persisting after 8-week PPI trial, with adult presentation typically being dysphagia and food impaction
eoe-adult.reasoning: This question requires analysis of clinical presentation, endoscopic findings, and histology to differentiate EoE from other conditions. The patient presents with classic EoE findings that must be distinguished from PPI-responsive eosinophilia and other esophageal disorders. This represents competent-level knowledge requiring independent application of diagnostic criteria and understanding of the PPI trial requirement for EoE diagnosis.
eoe-adult.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/eosinophilic-esophagitis

eoe-adult.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of progressive dysphagia to solids and two episodes of food impaction requiring endoscopic removal. He has a history of seasonal allergies and asthma. Upper endoscopy reveals concentric rings, longitudinal furrows, and white exudates throughout the esophagus. Esophageal biopsies show 22 eosinophils per high-power field. He completed an 8-week trial of high-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy without symptom improvement, and repeat biopsies continue to show 20 eosinophils per high-power field. The patient is highly motivated and asks about the most effective treatment option available.

options.text: Six-food elimination diet with systematic reintroduction
options.explanation: This is correct. The 6FED has the highest efficacy rate at 70-80% remission and is appropriate for motivated patients willing to follow strict dietary restrictions.

options.text: Swallowed fluticasone propionate inhaler twice daily
options.explanation: This is incorrect. While topical corticosteroids are effective (50-70% remission), they have lower efficacy than the 6FED which this motivated patient is asking about.

options.text: Dupilumab subcutaneous injections every two weeks
options.explanation: This is incorrect. While dupilumab showed 59% remission in the TREX trial, it has lower efficacy than the 6FED and is typically reserved for refractory cases.

options.text: Continue high-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The patient already failed an adequate 8-week PPI trial with persistent symptoms and eosinophilia, confirming true EoE rather than PPI-responsive disease.

options.text: Swallowed budesonide suspension twice daily
options.explanation: This is incorrect. While budesonide is an effective topical corticosteroid (50-70% remission), the 6FED offers higher efficacy rates for this motivated patient seeking the most effective option.

eoe-adult.explanation: This patient has confirmed eosinophilic esophagitis based on typical symptoms (dysphagia, food impaction), characteristic endoscopic findings (rings, furrows, exudates), persistent eosinophilia ≥15/hpf after adequate PPI trial, and lack of response to PPI therapy. The 6FED (six-food elimination diet) has the highest reported efficacy at 70-80% remission rates, making it the most effective treatment option for motivated patients. Topical corticosteroids achieve 50-70% remission, dupilumab achieved 59% remission in trials, and PPIs have the lowest efficacy at 30-40% and have already failed in this patient.
eoe-adult.key_concept: Six-food elimination diet has the highest efficacy (70-80%) among EoE treatment options
eoe-adult.reasoning: This question requires synthesis of clinical presentation, diagnostic criteria, and comparative treatment efficacy data for eosinophilic esophagitis. The learner must analyze multiple treatment options and their success rates to determine optimal management for a motivated patient, representing proficient-level clinical decision-making in a complex EoE case.
eoe-adult.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/eosinophilic-esophagitis

eoe-adult.stem: A 28-year-old man with a 3-year history of dysphagia and recurrent food impactions undergoes upper endoscopy, which reveals concentric rings throughout the esophagus and a focal stricture at 25 cm from the incisors. Esophageal biopsies show 22 eosinophils per high-power field despite an 8-week trial of high-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy. The patient reports significant improvement in swallowing after endoscopic dilation but returns 6 months later with worsening dysphagia. Repeat endoscopy shows similar esophageal rings and 18 eosinophils per high-power field in new biopsies.

options.text: Perform repeat endoscopic dilation as the primary treatment approach
options.explanation: Incorrect. While dilation improves symptoms temporarily, it does not address the underlying eosinophilic inflammation, leading to symptom recurrence as demonstrated in this case.

options.text: Initiate swallowed topical corticosteroid therapy in addition to periodic dilation
options.explanation: Correct. This patient has confirmed eosinophilic esophagitis with symptomatic stricture requiring both anti-inflammatory treatment (topical corticosteroids) for the underlying eosinophilic inflammation and dilation for mechanical obstruction.

options.text: Start systemic corticosteroid therapy to achieve rapid remission
options.explanation: Incorrect. Systemic corticosteroids are not standard first-line therapy for EoE due to significant side effects when topical alternatives are equally effective.

options.text: Recommend immediate surgical intervention for definitive stricture management
options.explanation: Incorrect. Surgery is not indicated as first-line management for EoE strictures when endoscopic dilation combined with medical therapy can effectively manage both inflammation and mechanical obstruction.

options.text: Increase proton pump inhibitor dose and continue monotherapy
options.explanation: Incorrect. This patient has already failed an adequate PPI trial with persistent eosinophilia ≥15/hpf, confirming true eosinophilic esophagitis rather than PPI-responsive disease.

eoe-adult.explanation: This patient has confirmed eosinophilic esophagitis based on >15 eosinophils/hpf after adequate PPI trial, characteristic endoscopic findings (rings, stricture), and typical presentation. The key teaching point is that dilation addresses the mechanical component (stricture) but does NOT treat the underlying eosinophilic inflammation. Symptom recurrence after dilation demonstrates why combination therapy is essential: anti-inflammatory treatment (topical corticosteroids, dietary elimination, or dupilumab) addresses the root cause while dilation provides symptomatic relief for established strictures. Monotherapy with either approach alone is inadequate for long-term management.
eoe-adult.key_concept: Dilation improves dysphagia from EoE strictures but does not treat underlying inflammation - combination with anti-inflammatory therapy is required
eoe-adult.reasoning: This complex EoE case requires analyzing the failure of monotherapy approaches and synthesizing the need for combination treatment addressing both inflammatory and mechanical components. The scenario demonstrates proficient-level understanding of EoE pathophysiology and management principles, requiring analysis of why dilation alone fails and integration of anti-inflammatory therapy concepts.
eoe-adult.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/eosinophilic-esophagitis

eoe-pediatric.stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with a 6-month history of dysphagia and food impaction episodes. His parents report he frequently complains of chest pain during meals and has developed aversion to solid foods, preferring liquids and soft foods. Physical examination reveals mild failure to thrive with weight at the 15th percentile (previously 50th percentile). Upper endoscopy shows linear furrows, white exudates, and mucosal fragility throughout the esophagus. Esophageal biopsy demonstrates 18 eosinophils per high-power field in the mid-esophagus and 22 eosinophils per high-power field in the distal esophagus. Proton pump inhibitor therapy for 8 weeks showed no clinical or histologic improvement.

options.text: Initiate elimination of cow's milk from the diet for 6-8 weeks
options.explanation: Correct. Cow's milk is the most common trigger food in pediatric EoE. Single food elimination starting with milk achieves remission in ~40% of cases and is the recommended first-line dietary approach before proceeding to more restrictive elimination diets.

options.text: Begin six-food elimination diet removing milk, wheat, egg, soy, nuts, and seafood
options.explanation: Incorrect. While 6FED is effective, current guidelines recommend starting with single food elimination (milk first) before proceeding to more restrictive diets, especially in children where nutritional monitoring is crucial.

options.text: Start elemental formula as the sole nutritional source
options.explanation: Incorrect. Although elemental formula achieves >90% histologic remission, it should be reserved for cases where targeted elimination diets fail, given its impact on quality of life and social functioning in children.

options.text: Prescribe topical fluticasone propionate swallowed twice daily
