penicillin-allergy-delabeling.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

scid-ada.stem: A 4-month-old male infant is brought to the pediatric clinic for evaluation of recurrent infections. He was born to consanguineous parents and has had three episodes of pneumonia, persistent oral thrush, and chronic diarrhea since birth. Physical examination reveals failure to thrive (weight <5th percentile), absent lymph nodes, and no palpable thymic shadow on chest radiograph. Laboratory studies show absolute lymphocyte count of 800/μL (normal 4000-13,500/μL), CD3+ T cells 45/μL (normal 2500-5500/μL), CD19+ B cells 12/μL (normal 1000-4000/μL), and NK cells 8/μL (normal 500-1200/μL). Newborn screening reveals undetectable T-cell receptor excision circles (TRECs). Enzymatic analysis demonstrates severely reduced adenosine deaminase activity in red blood cells.

options.text: Adenosine deaminase-deficient severe combined immunodeficiency
options.explanation: Correct. The constellation of T-B-NK- SCID, consanguineous parents (autosomal recessive inheritance), undetectable TRECs, and severely reduced ADA activity confirms ADA-SCID diagnosis.

options.text: X-linked severe combined immunodeficiency
options.explanation: Incorrect. X-linked SCID presents as T-B+NK- phenotype with normal B cell counts but absent T cells and NK cells. This patient has low B cells and normal ADA activity would be expected.

options.text: RAG1/RAG2-deficient severe combined immunodeficiency
options.explanation: Incorrect. RAG deficiency causes T-B-NK+ SCID with normal NK cell counts. This patient has severely reduced NK cells, and ADA activity would be normal in RAG deficiency.

options.text: JAK3-deficient severe combined immunodeficiency
options.explanation: Incorrect. JAK3 deficiency causes T-B+NK- SCID with normal B cell counts. This patient has severely reduced B cells, and ADA enzymatic activity would be normal.

options.text: Artemis-deficient severe combined immunodeficiency
options.explanation: Incorrect. Artemis deficiency causes T-B-NK+ SCID with normal NK cell counts and increased radiosensitivity. ADA enzymatic activity would be normal in Artemis deficiency.

scid-ada.explanation: This case demonstrates classic ADA-SCID presentation. ADA deficiency leads to dATP accumulation, causing lymphocyte apoptosis and resulting in T-B-NK- phenotype. Key diagnostic features include: consanguineous parents (autosomal recessive inheritance), severely reduced counts of all lymphocyte subsets, undetectable TRECs indicating absent T-cell development, and most importantly, severely reduced ADA enzymatic activity. The other SCID variants have different lymphocyte phenotypes and normal ADA activity.
scid-ada.key_concept: ADA-SCID diagnosis requires recognition of T-B-NK- phenotype, autosomal recessive inheritance, and reduced ADA enzymatic activity
scid-ada.reasoning: This complex case requires analysis of multiple clinical and laboratory findings to differentiate between various SCID subtypes. The question tests advanced knowledge of specific immunodeficiency disorders, requiring integration of inheritance patterns, lymphocyte phenotypes, and enzymatic deficiencies. This represents proficient-level knowledge as it involves managing complex diagnostic scenarios with multiple differential diagnoses.
scid-ada.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Severe Combined Immune Deficiency (SCID). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/severe-combined-immune-deficiency-scid

scid-ada.stem: A 3-month-old male infant is brought to the pediatrician for evaluation of recurrent infections. He was born at term to consanguineous parents and received routine immunizations including BCG vaccine at birth. Over the past 6 weeks, he has developed persistent oral thrush, two episodes of pneumonia requiring hospitalization, and chronic diarrhea with failure to thrive. Physical examination reveals an ill-appearing infant with weight below the 5th percentile, extensive oral candidiasis, and bilateral crackles on lung examination. Laboratory studies show absolute lymphocyte count of 800/μL (normal 4000-13500/μL). Flow cytometry reveals CD3+ T cells <100/μL (normal 2500-5600/μL), CD19+ B cells 50/μL (normal 600-2700/μL), and CD16/56+ NK cells <10/μL (normal 200-1200/μL). Newborn screening results are reviewed and show undetectable T-cell receptor excision circles (TRECs).

options.text: Adenosine deaminase deficiency with T-B-NK- severe combined immunodeficiency
options.explanation: Correct. The constellation of consanguineous parents, recurrent opportunistic infections, T-B-NK- phenotype, and undetectable TRECs strongly suggests ADA-SCID, the most common autosomal recessive form of SCID.

options.text: X-linked severe combined immunodeficiency due to IL2RG mutation
options.explanation: Incorrect. X-linked SCID typically presents with T-NK- B+ phenotype, not the T-B-NK- pattern seen here. Also, the autosomal recessive inheritance pattern (consanguineous parents) makes ADA-SCID more likely.

options.text: DiGeorge syndrome with thymic aplasia and T-cell deficiency
options.explanation: Incorrect. DiGeorge syndrome typically presents with cardiac anomalies, hypocalcemia, and facial dysmorphism, none of which are described. It also usually has normal B and NK cell counts.

options.text: Common variable immunodeficiency with hypogammaglobulinemia
options.explanation: Incorrect. CVID typically presents later in childhood or adulthood with normal T-cell counts and primarily affects antibody production. The severe T-cell lymphopenia and undetectable TRECs exclude this diagnosis.

options.text: Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome with thrombocytopenia and immunodeficiency
options.explanation: Incorrect. WAS typically presents with the classic triad of thrombocytopenia, eczema, and recurrent infections. The absence of bleeding or eczema and the T-B-NK- phenotype make this diagnosis unlikely.

scid-ada.explanation: This infant presents with classic ADA-SCID (adenosine deaminase deficiency). The key features include: consanguineous parents suggesting autosomal recessive inheritance, severe recurrent opportunistic infections from birth, failure to thrive, and the characteristic T-B-NK- immunophenotype with undetectable TRECs on newborn screening. ADA deficiency leads to accumulation of toxic metabolites (dATP) that cause apoptosis of all lymphocyte lineages. The undetectable TRECs indicate absent thymic T-cell production, which is pathognomonic for SCID. X-linked SCID would show T-NK- B+ pattern and affect only males in an X-linked pattern. DiGeorge syndrome typically has associated cardiac/facial anomalies and normal B/NK cells. CVID presents later with normal T-cell counts. Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome has thrombocytopenia and eczema as key features.
scid-ada.key_concept: ADA-SCID diagnosis based on T-B-NK- phenotype and undetectable TRECs in newborn screening
scid-ada.reasoning: This question requires analysis of complex clinical presentation, laboratory data, and newborn screening results to diagnose ADA-SCID. The T-B-NK- phenotype, undetectable TRECs, and consanguineous parents require synthesis of multiple clinical clues. This represents proficient-level knowledge as it involves distinguishing between different forms of SCID and understanding their distinct immunophenotypes and inheritance patterns.
scid-ada.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Severe Combined Immune Deficiency (SCID). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/severe-combined-immune-deficiency-scid

scid-ada.stem: A 4-month-old male infant is brought to the clinic by his parents for evaluation of recurrent infections. He was born at full term without complications but has had three episodes of pneumonia since birth, including one hospitalization for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia at 3 months of age. He also developed persistent oral thrush and chronic diarrhea. His growth has been poor, falling from the 50th percentile at birth to below the 5th percentile. On examination, he appears ill and underweight. Lymph nodes and tonsils are notably absent. Laboratory studies show: absolute lymphocyte count 800/μL (normal 4000-10000/μL), CD3+ T cells <50/μL (normal 2500-5500/μL), CD19+ B cells 150/μL (normal 600-2700/μL), and NK cells <20/μL (normal 200-1200/μL). Newborn screening results show undetectable T-cell receptor excision circles (TRECs). What is the most likely underlying mechanism of this patient's condition?

options.text: Deficiency of adenosine deaminase leading to toxic deoxyadenosine triphosphate accumulation
options.explanation: This is correct. The clinical presentation of severe recurrent infections including PCP, absent lymphoid tissue, and laboratory findings of T-B-NK SCID with undetectable TRECs are consistent with severe combined immunodeficiency. ADA deficiency causes accumulation of dATP, which is toxic to developing lymphocytes, resulting in this phenotype.

options.text: Defective DNA repair mechanisms causing increased radiosensitivity and immune dysfunction
options.explanation: This describes ataxia-telangiectasia or other DNA repair disorders. While these can cause immunodeficiency, they typically present with progressive cerebellar ataxia, telangiectasias, and elevated alpha-fetoprotein, which are not described in this case.

options.text: Absent or dysfunctional thymus gland preventing T-cell maturation and selection
options.explanation: This describes DiGeorge syndrome. While it can cause T-cell deficiency, it typically presents with cardiac defects, hypocalcemia, and characteristic facial features. The severe B-cell and NK cell deficiency seen here is not typical of DiGeorge syndrome.

options.text: Defective cytotoxic granule function leading to impaired lymphocyte and NK cell activity
options.explanation: This describes hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis or other cytotoxic defects. While these can present with recurrent infections, they typically manifest with fever, hepatosplenomegaly, and cytopenias rather than the profound lymphopenia seen here.

options.text: Abnormal complement cascade activation resulting in increased susceptibility to encapsulated bacteria
options.explanation: Complement deficiencies typically predispose to infections with encapsulated bacteria like Streptococcus and Neisseria, not the opportunistic infections like PCP seen in this case. The profound T-cell deficiency also points away from complement disorders.

scid-ada.explanation: This patient presents with classic ADA-SCID (adenosine deaminase-deficient severe combined immunodeficiency). The combination of severe recurrent infections including Pneumocystis pneumonia, absent lymphoid tissue, failure to thrive, and laboratory findings showing T-B-NK SCID with undetectable TRECs is pathognomonic. ADA deficiency leads to accumulation of deoxyadenosine triphosphate (dATP), which is toxic to developing lymphocytes, causing apoptosis and resulting in the absence of T cells, B cells, and NK cells. The other options describe different immunodeficiency mechanisms that don't match this specific clinical and laboratory presentation.
scid-ada.key_concept: ADA-SCID presents with severe recurrent infections including opportunistic pathogens due to absent T, B, and NK cells from toxic dATP accumulation
scid-ada.reasoning: This question requires high-level analysis of complex clinical data to distinguish ADA-SCID from other primary immunodeficiencies. The learner must synthesize multiple clinical findings (opportunistic infections, absent lymphoid tissue, specific laboratory abnormalities including undetectable TRECs) with underlying pathophysiology (dATP toxicity mechanism). This represents proficient-level knowledge requiring management of complex immunodeficiency scenarios.
scid-ada.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Severe Combined Immune Deficiency (SCID). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/severe-combined-immune-deficiency-scid

scid-ada.stem: A 4-month-old male infant born to consanguineous parents presents with failure to thrive, chronic diarrhea, and recurrent pneumonia. He was hospitalized at 2 months for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and cytomegalovirus pneumonitis. Laboratory studies reveal absolute lymphocyte count of 800/μL (normal 4000-13500/μL), CD3+ T cells <50/μL (normal 2500-5500/μL), CD19+ B cells 45/μL (normal 1000-2700/μL), and NK cells <10/μL (normal 500-1200/μL). Flow cytometry shows absent adenosine deaminase activity in red blood cells. The family asks about routine immunizations that were delayed due to his illness.

options.text: Administer inactivated vaccines only; avoid all live-attenuated vaccines including BCG
options.explanation: Correct. SCID patients have severely compromised cellular immunity and can develop fatal disseminated infections from live vaccines, particularly BCG.

options.text: Administer BCG vaccine immediately to prevent tuberculosis given his immunocompromised state
options.explanation: Incorrect. BCG is contraindicated in SCID as it can cause fatal disseminated mycobacterial infection due to absent T-cell immunity.

options.text: Delay all vaccines until after stem cell transplantation and immune reconstitution
options.explanation: Incorrect. While live vaccines should be avoided, inactivated vaccines can provide some protection and should be given when appropriate.

options.text: Administer live vaccines at reduced doses to minimize risk while providing protection
options.explanation: Incorrect. Any live vaccine, regardless of dose, poses significant risk of dissemination in SCID patients due to absent T-cell function.

options.text: Proceed with standard vaccination schedule as immunodeficiency is primarily humoral
options.explanation: Incorrect. ADA-SCID affects T, B, and NK cells (T-B-NK SCID), making live vaccines particularly dangerous due to absent cellular immunity.

scid-ada.explanation: This patient has ADA-SCID, evidenced by consanguineous parents (autosomal recessive inheritance), opportunistic infections (PCP, CMV), severe lymphopenia affecting all lineages (T-B-NK SCID), and absent adenosine deaminase activity. In SCID patients, live-attenuated vaccines including BCG are absolutely contraindicated due to risk of fatal disseminated infection. The absent T-cell immunity means patients cannot control even attenuated organisms. Inactivated vaccines can be safely administered and may provide some protection through maternal antibodies or residual immune function.
scid-ada.key_concept: Live vaccines including BCG are contraindicated in SCID due to risk of fatal disseminated infection
scid-ada.reasoning: This case requires analysis of complex clinical presentation (ADA-SCID with T-B-NK phenotype), interpretation of specialized laboratory data (flow cytometry, enzyme activity), and application of immunodeficiency principles to vaccination decisions. The contraindication of live vaccines in SCID represents proficient-level knowledge requiring understanding of pathophysiology and clinical implications.
scid-ada.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Severe Combined Immune Deficiency (SCID). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/severe-combined-immune-deficiency-scid

scid-ada.stem: A 4-month-old male infant presents to the pediatric ICU with fever, hepatosplenomegaly, and a diffuse erythematous rash. He was born at term and had normal newborn screening except for low T-cell receptor excision circles (TRECs). At 2 months, he developed recurrent otitis media and oral thrush. Flow cytometry shows CD3+ T cells <50/μL, CD19+ B cells <100/μL, and CD56+ NK cells <20/μL. Adenosine deaminase (ADA) enzyme activity is markedly reduced. Three days ago, he received a non-irradiated packed red blood cell transfusion for anemia. His current symptoms began 48 hours post-transfusion. Skin biopsy shows lymphocytic infiltration of the epidermis with keratinocyte apoptosis.

options.text: Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease from donor lymphocytes
options.explanation: Correct. SCID patients lack functional T cells to reject transfused donor lymphocytes, which can engraft and attack recipient tissues, causing GVHD with fever, rash, and hepatosplenomegaly.

options.text: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction due to minor blood group incompatibility
options.explanation: Incorrect. Delayed hemolytic reactions typically present with jaundice, hemoglobinuria, and falling hemoglobin, not the constellation of fever, rash, and hepatosplenomegaly with lymphocytic skin infiltration.

options.text: Cytomegalovirus reactivation following immunosuppressive blood transfusion
options.explanation: Incorrect. While CMV can cause fever and hepatosplenomegaly in SCID patients, the acute onset post-transfusion with characteristic skin findings and lymphocytic infiltration is more consistent with GVHD.

options.text: Acute allergic reaction to plasma proteins in the transfused blood product
options.explanation: Incorrect. Allergic transfusion reactions present with urticaria, bronchospasm, or anaphylaxis within minutes to hours, not delayed fever, hepatosplenomegaly, and lymphocytic skin infiltration.

options.text: Bacterial sepsis from contaminated blood product with secondary skin involvement
options.explanation: Incorrect. While bacterial contamination can cause fever, the specific pattern of erythematous rash with lymphocytic epidermal infiltration and keratinocyte apoptosis is pathognomonic for GVHD, not bacterial sepsis.

scid-ada.explanation: This patient has ADA-SCID (evidenced by low TRECs, absent T/B/NK cells, and reduced ADA activity) and developed transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD). In SCID, patients lack functional T cells to reject donor lymphocytes present in non-irradiated blood products. These donor lymphocytes can engraft and mount an immune response against recipient tissues, causing the classic triad of fever, rash, and hepatosplenomegaly. The skin biopsy findings of lymphocytic infiltration with keratinocyte apoptosis are pathognomonic for GVHD. This complication is preventable by using only irradiated blood products in SCID patients, which eliminates viable donor lymphocytes.
scid-ada.key_concept: Transfusion-associated GVHD in SCID patients receiving non-irradiated blood products
scid-ada.reasoning: This complex case requires deep understanding of SCID pathophysiology, recognition of transfusion-associated GVHD complications, and analysis of multiple clinical and laboratory findings. The scenario involves synthesis of immunodeficiency knowledge with transfusion medicine principles, representing proficient-level competency in managing complex primary immunodeficiency cases.
scid-ada.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Severe Combined Immune Deficiency (SCID). NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/severe-combined-immune-deficiency-scid

severe-allergic-reaction.stem: A 67-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes presents to the emergency department 45 minutes after developing acute onset of generalized urticaria, facial swelling, and difficulty breathing. He was started on amoxicillin-clavulanate 2 hours ago for suspected pneumonia. His blood pressure is 78/45 mmHg, heart rate is 125/min, and oxygen saturation is 88% on room air. Physical examination reveals diffuse wheals, angioedema of the lips and eyelids, and bilateral expiratory wheezing. His current medications include metoprolol, lisinopril, and metformin. After initial stabilization, which medication class was most likely responsible for this reaction?

options.text: Beta-lactam antibiotics
options.explanation: Correct. Beta-lactam antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins) are the most common cause of drug-induced anaphylaxis. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is a beta-lactam antibiotic and the temporal relationship strongly suggests this as the causative agent.

options.text: ACE inhibitors
options.explanation: Incorrect. While ACE inhibitors can cause angioedema, this typically presents without urticaria or systemic symptoms and develops gradually over months to years of therapy, not acutely after a new medication.

options.text: Beta-blockers
options.explanation: Incorrect. Beta-blockers do not typically cause anaphylaxis. They can worsen anaphylaxis by blocking epinephrine's effects and making treatment more difficult, but they are not a common cause of allergic reactions.

options.text: Biguanides
options.explanation: Incorrect. Metformin and other biguanides are not associated with anaphylactic reactions. The most common adverse effect of metformin is gastrointestinal upset, with rare cases of lactic acidosis.

options.text: Neuromuscular blocking agents
options.explanation: Incorrect. While neuromuscular blocking agents are the most common cause of perioperative anaphylaxis, this patient was not undergoing surgery and was not exposed to these medications in the emergency department setting.

severe-allergic-reaction.explanation: This patient presents with classic anaphylaxis (urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, hypotension) shortly after receiving amoxicillin-clavulanate. Beta-lactam antibiotics are the most common cause of drug-induced anaphylaxis overall. The temporal relationship (symptoms 2 hours after antibiotic administration) and classic presentation make this the most likely culprit. ACE inhibitors cause bradykinin-mediated angioedema without urticaria or systemic symptoms. Beta-blockers can complicate anaphylaxis treatment but don't cause it. Metformin doesn't cause allergic reactions. Neuromuscular blocking agents cause perioperative anaphylaxis, not relevant here.
severe-allergic-reaction.key_concept: Beta-lactam antibiotics are the most common cause of drug-induced anaphylaxis
severe-allergic-reaction.reasoning: This question requires analyzing a clinical scenario of drug-induced anaphylaxis to identify the most likely causative medication class. It tests knowledge of beta-lactam antibiotics as the leading cause of drug-induced anaphylaxis and requires differentiating between various medication classes and their associated adverse reactions. The analysis of temporal relationships and clinical presentation indicates this is at the analysis level of Bloom's taxonomy and represents competent-level medical knowledge for independent practice.
severe-allergic-reaction.source: NIAID/FAAN Anaphylaxis Diagnostic Criteria. National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/anaphylaxis

severe-allergic-reaction.stem: A 52-year-old man with hypertension presents to the emergency department 30 minutes after receiving intravenous cefazolin for a skin abscess at an urgent care clinic. He developed generalized urticaria, facial swelling, and difficulty breathing. His blood pressure is 85/45 mmHg, heart rate is 110/min, and oxygen saturation is 89% on room air. Physical examination reveals diffuse wheezing and angioedema of the lips and eyelids. An ECG shows new ST-segment depressions in leads V4-V6. Serum troponin I is elevated at 0.8 ng/mL. The patient's home medications include metoprolol and lisinopril.

options.text: Administer intramuscular epinephrine and prepare for emergency cardiac catheterization
options.explanation: Correct. This represents Kounis syndrome (anaphylaxis with concurrent cardiac ischemia). Epinephrine treats the underlying anaphylaxis and its cardiac manifestations, taking priority over cardiac interventions.

options.text: Obtain immediate cardiology consultation and delay epinephrine until after cardiac evaluation
options.explanation: Incorrect. Delaying epinephrine treatment of anaphylaxis to prioritize cardiac evaluation can be fatal. The cardiac ischemia is likely secondary to the anaphylactic reaction.

options.text: Administer intravenous nitroglycerin to treat the acute coronary syndrome
options.explanation: Incorrect. Nitroglycerin is a vasodilator that can worsen the hypotension associated with anaphylaxis and should be avoided in Kounis syndrome until anaphylaxis is treated.

options.text: Give intravenous methylprednisolone as the first-line treatment for this allergic reaction
options.explanation: Incorrect. Corticosteroids have a delayed onset of action and are not first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. Epinephrine is the immediate life-saving intervention required.

options.text: Administer intravenous glucagon followed by epinephrine due to beta-blocker therapy
options.explanation: Incorrect. While glucagon can help overcome epinephrine resistance from beta-blockers, epinephrine should be given first as the primary treatment. Glucagon is adjunctive therapy, not first-line.

severe-allergic-reaction.explanation: This patient has Kounis syndrome, characterized by anaphylaxis with concurrent acute coronary syndrome. The key principle is treating the underlying anaphylaxis first with epinephrine, as the cardiac ischemia is often secondary to the allergic reaction. The ECG changes and elevated troponin indicate cardiac involvement, but epinephrine addresses both the anaphylaxis and its cardiac manifestations. Vasodilators like nitroglycerin should be avoided as they can worsen anaphylactic hypotension. While the patient is on metoprolol, epinephrine remains first-line, with glucagon as potential adjunctive therapy if epinephrine response is inadequate.
severe-allergic-reaction.key_concept: Kounis syndrome requires treating anaphylaxis first with epinephrine before addressing cardiac manifestations
severe-allergic-reaction.reasoning: This is a complex anaphylaxis case involving Kounis syndrome (anaphylaxis with concurrent cardiac ischemia). It requires synthesis of knowledge about anaphylaxis management, drug allergy recognition, and understanding the pathophysiology of anaphylaxis-induced cardiac events. The case demands proficient-level clinical decision making to prioritize epinephrine over cardiac interventions despite obvious cardiac involvement. The complexity of managing competing clinical priorities (anaphylaxis vs. acute coronary syndrome) and understanding that the cardiac ischemia is secondary to the allergic reaction makes this a high-difficulty question requiring advanced clinical reasoning.
severe-allergic-reaction.source: NIAID/FAAN Anaphylaxis Diagnostic Criteria. National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/anaphylaxis

severe-allergic-reaction.stem: A 42-year-old man undergoes elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anesthesia. Five minutes after administration of rocuronium for muscle relaxation, he develops severe hypotension (BP 70/40 mmHg), tachycardia (HR 130/min), and diffuse erythematous rash. His oxygen saturation drops to 88% despite mechanical ventilation. The anesthesiologist notes bilateral wheezing on chest auscultation and significant facial edema. His medical history includes hypertension treated with metoprolol and seasonal allergies managed with antihistamines. He has no known drug allergies and has undergone two previous surgeries without complications.

options.text: Neuromuscular blocking agent-induced anaphylaxis
options.explanation: Correct. NMBAs are the most common cause of perioperative anaphylaxis. The timing (5 minutes after rocuronium), classic triad of hypotension, bronchospasm, and cutaneous manifestations strongly suggest NMBA anaphylaxis.

options.text: Latex-induced allergic reaction
options.explanation: Incorrect. While latex is a common cause of perioperative anaphylaxis, the timeline doesn't fit as latex exposure would typically occur earlier during positioning/preparation, not specifically after NMBA administration.

options.text: Chlorhexidine-induced anaphylaxis
options.explanation: Incorrect. Chlorhexidine is indeed a common perioperative allergen, but the temporal relationship with rocuronium administration makes NMBA anaphylaxis more likely. Chlorhexidine exposure would occur during skin preparation.

options.text: Propofol infusion syndrome
options.explanation: Incorrect. Propofol infusion syndrome typically occurs with prolonged high-dose propofol infusion and presents with metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, and cardiac dysfunction, not the acute allergic manifestations described.

options.text: Malignant hyperthermia
options.explanation: Incorrect. While malignant hyperthermia is a perioperative emergency, it typically presents with hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, and hypercarbia. The cutaneous manifestations and timeline don't fit this diagnosis.

severe-allergic-reaction.explanation: This patient presents with classic perioperative anaphylaxis occurring 5 minutes after rocuronium administration. Neuromuscular blocking agents are the most common cause of anaphylaxis in the operating room, accounting for approximately 60% of cases. The presentation includes the classic triad of cardiovascular collapse (hypotension, tachycardia), respiratory compromise (bronchospasm, hypoxemia), and cutaneous manifestations (rash, angioedema). The temporal relationship with NMBA administration is highly suggestive. His beta-blocker therapy may complicate treatment as it can cause epinephrine resistance, potentially requiring glucagon administration. Immediate treatment should include epinephrine, IV fluids, and supportive care.
severe-allergic-reaction.key_concept: Neuromuscular blocking agents are the most common cause of perioperative anaphylaxis
severe-allergic-reaction.reasoning: This question requires analysis of a complex perioperative anaphylaxis case involving temporal relationships, clinical presentation patterns, and differential diagnosis. The learner must distinguish between multiple perioperative complications and understand that NMBAs are the leading cause of perioperative anaphylaxis. This represents competent-level medical knowledge requiring independent clinical reasoning to analyze the timeline and symptoms.
severe-allergic-reaction.source: NIAID/FAAN Anaphylaxis Diagnostic Criteria. National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/anaphylaxis

severe-allergic-reaction.stem: A 67-year-old man with hypertension and coronary artery disease develops acute onset of generalized urticaria, facial swelling, and wheezing 15 minutes after receiving intravenous cefazolin during preoperative preparation for knee replacement surgery. His blood pressure drops from 140/85 mmHg to 80/45 mmHg, and his heart rate increases to 120/min. He is taking metoprolol 50 mg twice daily for his cardiac condition. After administering intramuscular epinephrine 0.3 mg, his blood pressure remains at 75/40 mmHg despite adequate intravenous fluid resuscitation.

options.text: Administer intravenous glucagon 1-2 mg bolus followed by continuous infusion
options.explanation: Correct. Beta-blockers can cause epinephrine resistance in anaphylaxis by blocking beta-adrenergic receptors. Glucagon bypasses this blockade by acting through different receptors to increase cardiac contractility and heart rate.

options.text: Increase epinephrine dose to 0.5 mg intramuscularly and repeat every 5 minutes
options.explanation: Incorrect. While epinephrine is first-line, simply increasing the dose without addressing beta-blocker interference is less effective than using glucagon to overcome the receptor blockade.

options.text: Switch to intravenous norepinephrine infusion for vasopressor support
options.explanation: Incorrect. Norepinephrine primarily acts on alpha-receptors and would not overcome beta-blocker-induced epinephrine resistance. Glucagon is specifically indicated for this scenario.

options.text: Administer intravenous methylprednisolone 125 mg for refractory hypotension
options.explanation: Incorrect. Corticosteroids have a delayed onset of action and do not address the immediate problem of beta-blocker interference with epinephrine effectiveness.

options.text: Give sublingual nitroglycerin to improve cardiac output and blood pressure
options.explanation: Incorrect. Nitroglycerin is a vasodilator that would worsen hypotension in anaphylaxis. It's contraindicated in this setting and doesn't address epinephrine resistance.

severe-allergic-reaction.explanation: This patient has perioperative anaphylaxis to cefazolin with refractory hypotension despite epinephrine and IV fluids. The key clinical clue is his concurrent beta-blocker therapy (metoprolol). Beta-blockers can cause epinephrine resistance by blocking beta-adrenergic receptors, preventing epinephrine's positive inotropic and chronotropic effects. Glucagon bypasses this blockade by acting through glucagon receptors to activate adenylyl cyclase via a different pathway, increasing cardiac contractility and heart rate. The other options either don't address the underlying beta-blocker interference or could worsen the patient's condition.
severe-allergic-reaction.key_concept: Glucagon administration for epinephrine-resistant anaphylaxis in patients on beta-blocker therapy
severe-allergic-reaction.reasoning: This complex anaphylaxis case requires proficient-level knowledge to recognize epinephrine resistance due to beta-blocker therapy and the specific indication for glucagon. It tests analytical thinking to identify the underlying mechanism and select appropriate treatment beyond standard epinephrine administration, representing advanced clinical decision-making in emergency allergy management.
severe-allergic-reaction.source: NIAID/FAAN Anaphylaxis Diagnostic Criteria. National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/anaphylaxis

severe-allergic-reaction.stem: A 45-year-old man with a history of hypertension presents to the emergency department 20 minutes after receiving intravenous cefazolin for surgical prophylaxis before knee arthroscopy. He developed generalized urticaria, facial swelling, wheezing, and hypotension (blood pressure 85/50 mmHg). He was treated with intramuscular epinephrine, intravenous fluids, and corticosteroids with resolution of symptoms. His medications include lisinopril and metoprolol. He has no known drug allergies but recalls having a mild rash years ago after taking 'some antibiotic' that he cannot identify. He is scheduled for discharge and asks about future antibiotic use.

options.text: He can safely receive cephalexin for future infections since it is a different generation cephalosporin
options.explanation: Incorrect. Cross-reactivity exists between cefazolin (first-generation) and cephalexin (first-generation cephalosporins), and formal allergy evaluation is needed before any beta-lactam use.

options.text: He should avoid all beta-lactam antibiotics until formal allergy evaluation by an allergist
options.explanation: Correct. After confirmed beta-lactam anaphylaxis, all beta-lactams (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems, monobactams) should be avoided until formal evaluation including skin testing and possible drug challenge.

options.text: He can receive aztreonam safely as it has no cross-reactivity with cephalosporins
options.explanation: Incorrect. While aztreonam (monobactam) has lower cross-reactivity risk, it is still a beta-lactam and should be avoided until formal allergy evaluation after anaphylaxis.

options.text: He should receive a corticosteroid premedication before any future cephalosporin use
options.explanation: Incorrect. Premedication does not prevent anaphylaxis and may mask early symptoms. The culprit drug class should be avoided until proper allergy evaluation.

options.text: He can safely receive penicillin-based antibiotics since his reaction was specifically to cefazolin
options.explanation: Incorrect. Cross-reactivity exists between all beta-lactam antibiotics including penicillins and cephalosporins. All should be avoided until formal evaluation.

severe-allergic-reaction.explanation: This patient experienced cefazolin-induced anaphylaxis, confirmed by the constellation of urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, and hypotension occurring within minutes of drug administration. After drug anaphylaxis, the culprit drug and all cross-reactive medications must be avoided until formal allergist evaluation. Beta-lactam antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems, and monobactams) have significant cross-reactivity potential. Formal evaluation includes detailed history, skin testing, and potentially graded drug challenges under controlled conditions. Premedication does not prevent anaphylaxis and may mask early warning symptoms. The other options incorrectly suggest that certain beta-lactams are safe or that premedication provides adequate protection.
severe-allergic-reaction.key_concept: After drug anaphylaxis: avoid culprit AND cross-reactive drugs until formal evaluation. Allergist referral mandatory.
severe-allergic-reaction.reasoning: This question tests application of drug allergy management principles following confirmed anaphylaxis. It requires understanding beta-lactam cross-reactivity patterns and the appropriate clinical response (avoid all beta-lactams, refer to allergist). The scenario represents a common clinical situation requiring competent-level decision-making about drug safety and allergy evaluation protocols.
severe-allergic-reaction.source: NIAID/FAAN Anaphylaxis Diagnostic Criteria. National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/anaphylaxis

severe-asthma-biologics.stem: A 42-year-old woman with severe persistent asthma presents for biologic therapy evaluation. Despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroids, LABA, and LAMA, she requires 15 mg daily prednisone for control and has had 4 exacerbations requiring hospitalization in the past year. She has comorbid chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps and moderate atopic dermatitis affecting 8% body surface area. Laboratory studies show blood eosinophils 120 cells/μL and fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) 18 ppb. Skin prick testing is positive for dust mites and tree pollens.

options.text: Dupilumab subcutaneous injection every 2 weeks
options.explanation: Correct. Despite eosinophils <150, this patient is oral corticosteroid-dependent, which eliminates the eosinophil threshold requirement for dupilumab. Additionally, dupilumab is the only biologic approved for all three conditions (asthma, CRSwNP, and atopic dermatitis).

options.text: Mepolizumab subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. Blood eosinophils are 120 cells/μL, below the required ≥300 threshold for anti-IL-5 therapy. Additionally, mepolizumab is not approved for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps.

options.text: Benralizumab subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks after loading
options.explanation: Incorrect. Blood eosinophils are 120 cells/μL, below the required ≥300 threshold for anti-IL-5 receptor therapy. Benralizumab is also not approved for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps.

options.text: Tezepelumab subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. While tezepelumab has no biomarker threshold, it is less preferred when clear Type 2 inflammation is present. This patient has multiple Type 2 comorbidities and would benefit more from targeted Type 2 therapy.

options.text: Omalizumab subcutaneous injection every 2-4 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. While the patient has positive skin tests, omalizumab is not the optimal choice for severe eosinophilic asthma with multiple Type 2 comorbidities. It also lacks approval for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps and atopic dermatitis.

severe-asthma-biologics.explanation: This patient requires biologic therapy for severe asthma with multiple Type 2 comorbidities. The key insight is that dupilumab's biomarker thresholds (blood eosinophils ≥150 OR FeNO ≥25) are waived in oral corticosteroid-dependent patients. Since she requires daily prednisone, any eosinophil count qualifies her for dupilumab. Furthermore, dupilumab is uniquely approved for asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, AND atopic dermatitis, making it ideal for treating all her conditions simultaneously. Anti-IL-5 therapies require eosinophils ≥300 and lack CRSwNP approval. Tezepelumab, while having no biomarker requirements, is less optimal when clear Type 2 biomarkers and comorbidities are present.
severe-asthma-biologics.key_concept: Dupilumab biomarker thresholds are waived in oral corticosteroid-dependent asthma patients, and it provides the broadest comorbidity coverage for Type 2 conditions
severe-asthma-biologics.reasoning: This complex case requires advanced knowledge of biologic therapy selection for severe asthma with multiple Type 2 comorbidities. The question tests understanding of specific biomarker thresholds, exceptions for oral corticosteroid-dependent patients, and comparative indications across different biologics. This represents proficient-level knowledge requiring analysis of multiple variables to select optimal therapy.
severe-asthma-biologics.source: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute. 2020 Focused Updates to the Asthma Management Guidelines. NIH/NHLBI, 2020. Available: nhlbi.nih.gov

severe-asthma-biologics.stem: A 42-year-old woman with severe persistent asthma presents for biologic therapy consideration after remaining poorly controlled despite maximum inhaled therapy and prednisone 10 mg daily for 8 months. She reports daily wheezing, chest tightness, and uses her rescue inhaler 6-8 times daily. Her comorbidities include chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps requiring three previous surgeries, moderate atopic dermatitis on her arms and legs, and aspirin sensitivity with bronchospasm. Laboratory results show peripheral eosinophil count of 280 cells/μL and fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) of 45 ppb. Skin testing reveals multiple aeroallergen sensitivities including dust mites, tree pollens, and molds.

options.text: Dupilumab subcutaneous injection
options.explanation: Correct. Dupilumab is the only biologic approved for asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, and atopic dermatitis, making it ideal for this patient with multiple Type 2 inflammatory conditions.

options.text: Mepolizumab subcutaneous injection
options.explanation: Incorrect. While effective for eosinophilic asthma (eos ≥300), mepolizumab is not approved for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps or atopic dermatitis, missing key comorbidities.

options.text: Benralizumab subcutaneous injection
options.explanation: Incorrect. Anti-IL-5 receptor antagonist effective for eosinophilic asthma but not approved for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps or atopic dermatitis in this clinical context.

options.text: Tezepelumab subcutaneous injection
options.explanation: Incorrect. While tezepelumab has no eosinophil threshold requirement, it's less preferred when clear Type 2 biomarkers are present and doesn't address the nasal polyps or dermatitis.

options.text: Omalizumab subcutaneous injection
options.explanation: Incorrect. Anti-IgE therapy is appropriate for allergic asthma but not approved for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps in AERD or for atopic dermatitis treatment.

severe-asthma-biologics.explanation: This patient has severe eosinophilic asthma with multiple Type 2 inflammatory comorbidities. Dupilumab (anti-IL-4/IL-13) is the only biologic approved for all three conditions: asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, and atopic dermatitis. The patient meets dupilumab criteria with eosinophils ≥150 and FeNO ≥25. Anti-IL-5 therapies (mepolizumab/benralizumab) are not approved for nasal polyps in AERD. Tezepelumab, while effective, is less preferred when clear Type 2 biomarkers are present and doesn't address comorbidities. Omalizumab targets IgE-mediated pathways but lacks approval for nasal polyps in AERD context.
severe-asthma-biologics.key_concept: Dupilumab is only biologic approved for asthma + CRSwNP + atopic dermatitis + EoE — broadest comorbidity coverage
severe-asthma-biologics.reasoning: This complex case requires synthesis of multiple factors: severe asthma management, biologic therapy selection, and understanding of overlapping Type 2 inflammatory conditions. The learner must integrate knowledge of different biologic mechanisms, FDA approvals for multiple conditions, and patient-specific factors to select optimal therapy. This represents proficient-level decision-making for complex asthma management with comorbidities.
severe-asthma-biologics.source: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute. 2020 Focused Updates to the Asthma Management Guidelines. NIH/NHLBI, 2020. Available: nhlbi.nih.gov

severe-asthma-biologics.stem: A 42-year-old woman with severe persistent asthma presents for evaluation of biologic therapy. Despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonist, and leukotriene receptor antagonist, she experiences weekly exacerbations requiring oral corticosteroids. She has concomitant chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps and reports severe reactions to aspirin including bronchospasm and nasal congestion. Physical examination reveals bilateral nasal polyps and diffuse expiratory wheeze. Spirometry shows FEV1 55% predicted with minimal bronchodilator response. Laboratory studies reveal peripheral eosinophil count of 450 cells/μL, total IgE 380 IU/mL, and fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) 65 ppb. Skin testing is positive for multiple environmental allergens.

options.text: Dupilumab subcutaneous injection every 2 weeks
options.explanation: Correct choice. Dupilumab is the only biologic approved for asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, and has efficacy in AERD. Patient meets criteria with eosinophils ≥150 and FeNO ≥25.

options.text: Mepolizumab subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. While patient has eosinophils ≥300, mepolizumab is not FDA-approved for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, particularly in AERD context.

options.text: Benralizumab subcutaneous injection every 8 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. Although patient meets eosinophil threshold ≥300, benralizumab lacks approval for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps and is not indicated for AERD.

options.text: Tezepelumab subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. While tezepelumab has no biomarker threshold requirement, it is less preferred when clear Type 2 biomarkers are present, as in this patient.

options.text: Omalizumab subcutaneous injection every 2-4 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. Patient's total IgE of 380 IU/mL is within range, but omalizumab is not first-line for this phenotype with high eosinophils and FeNO.

severe-asthma-biologics.explanation: This patient has severe eosinophilic asthma with aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD) and chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps. Dupilumab is the optimal choice because it is the only biologic approved for all three conditions: asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, and has demonstrated efficacy in AERD. The patient meets dupilumab criteria with eosinophils ≥150 (450) and FeNO ≥25 (65). Anti-IL-5 agents (mepolizumab, benralizumab) require eosinophils ≥300 but are not approved for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps. Tezepelumab has no biomarker threshold but is less preferred when clear Type 2 biomarkers are present. Omalizumab could be considered but is not optimal for this high-eosinophil, high-FeNO phenotype with nasal polyps.
severe-asthma-biologics.key_concept: Dupilumab provides broadest comorbidity coverage for Type 2 high asthma with chronic rhinosinusitis and AERD
severe-asthma-biologics.reasoning: This question requires advanced knowledge of biologic therapy selection for complex severe asthma phenotypes. The learner must analyze multiple biomarkers (eosinophils, FeNO, IgE), understand FDA approvals for different comorbidities (asthma, CRS with nasal polyps, AERD), and synthesize this information to select optimal therapy. This represents proficient-level milestone requiring management of complex cases with multiple overlapping conditions.
severe-asthma-biologics.source: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute. 2020 Focused Updates to the Asthma Management Guidelines. NIH/NHLBI, 2020. Available: nhlbi.nih.gov

severe-asthma-biologics.stem: A 42-year-old woman with severe persistent asthma presents for biologic therapy consideration. Despite maximized inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists, and leukotriene modifiers, she requires oral prednisone 15 mg daily and has had 3 hospitalizations in the past year. She reports chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps requiring multiple surgeries, and moderate atopic dermatitis on her arms and legs. Laboratory studies show blood eosinophils 420 cells/μL and fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) 45 ppb. Skin prick tests are positive for multiple aeroallergens including dust mites, pollens, and molds.

options.text: Dupilumab subcutaneous injection every 2 weeks
options.explanation: Correct. Dupilumab is indicated for severe asthma with Type 2 inflammation (eos ≥150 OR FeNO ≥25, both met here). Uniquely approved for the triad of asthma + CRSwNP + atopic dermatitis, providing optimal coverage for this patient's comorbidities.

options.text: Mepolizumab subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. While mepolizumab is appropriate for severe eosinophilic asthma (eos ≥300 met), it is not approved for CRSwNP and provides no benefit for atopic dermatitis, missing key comorbidity coverage.

options.text: Benralizumab subcutaneous injection every 8 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. Though benralizumab is suitable for severe eosinophilic asthma (eos ≥300 met), it lacks approval for CRSwNP and atopic dermatitis, making it suboptimal for this patient's multiple Type 2 conditions.

options.text: Tezepelumab subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. While tezepelumab has no biomarker threshold requirements, it is less preferred when clear Type 2 biomarkers are present (elevated eos and FeNO here). Better reserved for non-Type 2 asthma phenotypes.

options.text: Omalizumab subcutaneous injection every 2-4 weeks
options.explanation: Incorrect. Although omalizumab is appropriate for allergic asthma with positive skin tests, it requires specific IgE and weight-based dosing criteria, and provides limited coverage for CRSwNP compared to other options available.

severe-asthma-biologics.explanation: This patient has severe Type 2 high asthma with clear biomarker evidence (eosinophils 420 cells/μL and FeNO 45 ppb), meeting dupilumab thresholds (eos ≥150 OR FeNO ≥25). Crucially, dupilumab is the only biologic approved for all three of her conditions: severe asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, and atopic dermatitis. Anti-IL-5 therapies (mepolizumab, benralizumab) meet eosinophil thresholds but lack CRSwNP approval. Tezepelumab, while having no biomarker requirements, is less preferred when clear Type 2 inflammation is present. Omalizumab requires specific IgE/weight criteria and has limited polyp benefits.
severe-asthma-biologics.key_concept: Dupilumab provides optimal coverage for severe Type 2 asthma with multiple comorbidities (CRSwNP, atopic dermatitis) when biomarker thresholds are met
severe-asthma-biologics.reasoning: This question requires advanced synthesis of biologic therapy selection for severe asthma with multiple comorbidities. The learner must integrate biomarker interpretation (eosinophils, FeNO), understand different biologic mechanisms and indications, and synthesize the optimal choice based on the patient's complex phenotype including asthma, CRSwNP, and atopic dermatitis. This represents proficient-level medical knowledge requiring management of complex, atypical cases with multiple treatment considerations.
severe-asthma-biologics.source: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute. 2020 Focused Updates to the Asthma Management Guidelines. NIH/NHLBI, 2020. Available: nhlbi.nih.gov

severe-asthma-biologics.stem: A 42-year-old woman with a 15-year history of asthma presents for evaluation of worsening symptoms despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroids and LABA. She reports severe nasal congestion with anosmia, requires daily oral prednisone 15 mg for symptom control, and has had three polypectomies. She avoids aspirin after experiencing severe bronchospasm and nasal congestion when taking it for headaches. Pulmonary function tests show FEV1 45% predicted. Laboratory studies reveal blood eosinophils 420 cells/μL and fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) 65 ppb. CT sinuses demonstrates pansinusitis with extensive nasal polyposis. She also has moderate atopic dermatitis affecting her arms and face.

options.text: Dupilumab therapy
options.explanation: Correct choice - addresses asthma, CRSwNP, and atopic dermatitis simultaneously in this AERD patient with Type 2 high inflammation

options.text: Mepolizumab therapy
options.explanation: Incorrect - while eosinophils >300 support anti-IL-5 therapy, mepolizumab is not FDA-approved for CRSwNP treatment in AERD

options.text: Benralizumab therapy
options.explanation: Incorrect - though effective for eosinophilic asthma, benralizumab lacks FDA approval for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps

options.text: Tezepelumab therapy
options.explanation: Incorrect - while effective regardless of biomarker levels, less optimal when clear Type 2 inflammation markers are present

options.text: Omalizumab therapy
options.explanation: Incorrect - requires elevated IgE levels and positive specific IgE to perennial allergens, which are not documented in this case

severe-asthma-biologics.explanation: This patient has aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD) with the classic triad of asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps (CRSwNP), and aspirin sensitivity. The mechanism involves COX-1 inhibition blocking prostaglandin E2 synthesis, removing the brake on mast cell leukotriene production, leading to LTC4/LTD4 surge causing bronchospasm and nasal congestion. Her Type 2 high inflammation is evidenced by blood eosinophils >150 and FeNO >25. Dupilumab (anti-IL-4/IL-13) is uniquely positioned as the only biologic approved for all three conditions present: severe asthma, CRSwNP, and atopic dermatitis. While mepolizumab and benralizumab target IL-5 and are effective for eosinophilic asthma (eos >300), they lack FDA approval for CRSwNP. Tezepelumab works regardless of biomarkers but is less preferred when clear Type 2 markers exist. Omalizumab requires documented IgE-mediated allergic asthma.
severe-asthma-biologics.key_concept: Dupilumab provides optimal treatment for AERD patients with multiple Type 2 inflammatory comorbidities due to its broad FDA approvals
severe-asthma-biologics.reasoning: This complex case requires analysis of AERD pathophysiology and synthesis of multiple clinical findings (asthma severity, CRSwNP, aspirin sensitivity, Type 2 biomarkers, atopic dermatitis) to select appropriate biologic therapy. The question demands proficient-level knowledge of biologic mechanisms, FDA approvals, and treatment algorithms for severe asthma with comorbidities, going beyond routine patient care scenarios.
severe-asthma-biologics.source: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute. 2020 Focused Updates to the Asthma Management Guidelines. NIH/NHLBI, 2020. Available: nhlbi.nih.gov

sjs-ten.stem: A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of progressive skin eruption and fever. He started trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 10 days ago for a urinary tract infection. Physical examination reveals temperature 38.9°C, widespread erythematous macules with scattered blisters covering approximately 25% of his body surface area, and extensive oral ulcerations with bleeding. Conjunctival injection and genital erosions are present. Gentle lateral pressure on intact skin produces extension of blisters. Laboratory studies show leukocytosis with eosinophilia.

options.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome with toxic epidermal necrolysis overlap
options.explanation: Correct. 25% BSA involvement falls in the 10-30% range defining SJS-TEN overlap syndrome. Positive Nikolsky sign, drug causation, and extensive mucosal involvement are characteristic.

options.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome
options.explanation: Incorrect. SJS is defined as <10% BSA involvement, but this patient has 25% BSA affected, which exceeds the SJS threshold.

options.text: Toxic epidermal necrolysis
options.explanation: Incorrect. TEN requires >30% BSA involvement, but this patient has 25% BSA affected, which is below the TEN threshold.

options.text: Erythema multiforme major
options.explanation: Incorrect. EM major typically has classic 3-zone target lesions, is usually infection-triggered (especially HSV), has limited mucosal involvement, and negative Nikolsky sign.

options.text: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
options.explanation: Incorrect. SSSS typically occurs in children, has a different morphology without mucosal involvement, and is caused by staphylococcal toxins rather than drug hypersensitivity.

sjs-ten.explanation: This patient has SJS-TEN overlap syndrome based on 25% BSA involvement (10-30% range), positive Nikolsky sign, drug causation (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole), and extensive mucosal involvement. SJS alone would be <10% BSA, while TEN would be >30% BSA. The positive Nikolsky sign, drug trigger, and severe mucosal involvement distinguish this from erythema multiforme major, which typically has negative Nikolsky sign and limited mucosal involvement.
sjs-ten.key_concept: SJS-TEN classification based on body surface area involvement: SJS <10%, overlap 10-30%, TEN >30%
sjs-ten.reasoning: This question tests knowledge of severe cutaneous adverse drug reactions, specifically the classification system for SJS-TEN spectrum based on BSA involvement. Requires analysis of clinical features, drug causation, and application of diagnostic criteria to distinguish between overlapping conditions. The 25% BSA involvement placing it in the overlap category requires understanding of the classification system rather than simple recall.
sjs-ten.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

sjs-ten.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, malaise, and progressive skin eruption that began 10 days after starting phenytoin for newly diagnosed epilepsy. Physical examination reveals widespread erythematous macules with epidermal detachment involving 25% of her body surface area, including the face, trunk, and extremities. Mucosal involvement is present in the mouth, eyes, and genital areas. Gentle lateral pressure on apparently normal skin causes separation of the epidermis. Laboratory studies show leukocytosis with atypical lymphocytes. Skin biopsy demonstrates full-thickness epidermal necrosis with minimal dermal inflammation.

options.text: Granulysin released from activated cytotoxic T lymphocytes and natural killer cells
options.explanation: Correct. Granulysin is the principal cytotoxic mediator in SJS/TEN, released by CD8+ T cells and NK cells, causing keratinocyte apoptosis and epidermal detachment.

options.text: Histamine degranulation from mast cells and basophils causing vasodilation
options.explanation: Incorrect. Histamine mediates immediate hypersensitivity reactions (Type I), not the delayed cytotoxic response seen in SJS/TEN.

options.text: IgE-mediated crosslinking leading to complement activation and inflammation
options.explanation: Incorrect. SJS/TEN is a T cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity reaction (Type IV), not an IgE-mediated immediate reaction.

options.text: Immune complex deposition triggering neutrophil activation and tissue damage
options.explanation: Incorrect. This describes Type III hypersensitivity. SJS/TEN involves direct cytotoxic T cell killing of keratinocytes, not immune complex-mediated damage.

options.text: Antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity against epidermal basement membrane proteins
options.explanation: Incorrect. This mechanism occurs in autoimmune blistering diseases like pemphigoid. SJS/TEN involves direct cellular cytotoxicity without antibody targeting of basement membrane.

sjs-ten.explanation: This patient has Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN) overlap syndrome based on 25% BSA involvement, positive Nikolsky sign, and drug causation. The principal pathophysiologic mechanism is granulysin release from activated CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes and NK cells, which induces keratinocyte apoptosis and epidermal detachment. This is a Type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction, not involving histamine (Type I), IgE-mediated mechanisms (Type I), immune complexes (Type III), or antibody-mediated cytotoxicity against basement membrane proteins (seen in pemphigoid disorders).
sjs-ten.key_concept: Granulysin from CD8+ T cells and NK cells is the principal cytotoxin in SJS/TEN pathogenesis
sjs-ten.reasoning: This question requires understanding the complex pathophysiology of SJS/TEN, specifically the role of granulysin in cytotoxic T cell-mediated keratinocyte death. It tests ability to distinguish between different hypersensitivity mechanisms and analyze the cellular processes underlying severe cutaneous adverse reactions to drugs. The clinical scenario is complex with multiple diagnostic features requiring synthesis of knowledge.
sjs-ten.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

sjs-ten.stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, malaise, and progressive skin eruption that began 10 days after starting allopurinol for gout. She reports severe oral pain and difficulty swallowing. Physical examination reveals temperature 38.9°C, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, and heart rate 105 bpm. Skin examination shows widespread erythematous areas with flaccid bullae and areas of epidermal detachment covering approximately 35% of her body surface area. Mucous membrane involvement includes painful erosions of the oral cavity, conjunctiva, and genital mucosa. When gentle lateral pressure is applied to the edge of a blister, the epidermis easily separates from the dermis. Laboratory studies show leukocytosis with left shift and elevated liver enzymes.

options.text: Toxic epidermal necrolysis with positive Nikolsky sign
options.explanation: Correct. >30% BSA involvement, drug-induced, extensive mucosal involvement, and positive Nikolsky sign (epidermis separates with lateral pressure) are characteristic of TEN.

options.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome with negative Nikolsky sign
options.explanation: Incorrect. While SJS can be drug-induced with mucosal involvement, this patient has >30% BSA involvement (indicating TEN) and demonstrates a positive Nikolsky sign.

options.text: Erythema multiforme major with target lesions
options.explanation: Incorrect. EM major typically shows classic three-zone target lesions, is usually infection-related (especially HSV), has limited mucosal involvement, and demonstrates a negative Nikolsky sign.

options.text: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome with desquamation
options.explanation: Incorrect. While SSSS can present with positive Nikolsky sign, it typically occurs in children, lacks mucosal involvement, and is caused by staphylococcal toxins rather than drug hypersensitivity.

options.text: Pemphigus vulgaris with acantholysis
options.explanation: Incorrect. While pemphigus can present with positive Nikolsky sign and mucosal involvement, it is an autoimmune blistering disease with different histology and is not associated with recent drug initiation.

sjs-ten.explanation: This patient presents with toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) based on >30% body surface area involvement, drug-induced etiology (allopurinol), extensive mucosal involvement, and positive Nikolsky sign. The Nikolsky sign is positive in TEN (as well as pemphigus and SSSS) where gentle lateral pressure causes epidermis to separate from dermis. This distinguishes TEN from SJS and erythema multiforme major, which have negative Nikolsky signs. The >30% BSA involvement differentiates this from SJS (<10% BSA) or SJS-TEN overlap (10-30% BSA). The drug-induced etiology and extensive mucosal involvement help distinguish from other blistering disorders.
sjs-ten.key_concept: Nikolsky sign differentiation between severe cutaneous adverse reactions
sjs-ten.reasoning: This complex case requires analysis of multiple clinical features (BSA involvement, mucosal involvement, Nikolsky sign, drug history) to differentiate between severe cutaneous adverse reactions (TEN vs SJS vs EM major vs other blistering disorders). The proficient level is appropriate as it involves managing a complex, potentially life-threatening drug reaction with multiple differential considerations requiring advanced diagnostic reasoning.
sjs-ten.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

sjs-ten.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, malaise, and progressive skin eruption. She started phenytoin 10 days ago for new-onset seizures. Physical examination reveals temperature 38.9°C, conjunctival injection with purulent discharge, erosions involving the oral mucosa and genital area, and widespread dusky erythematous lesions with ill-defined borders covering 15% of her body surface area. Gentle lateral pressure on an intact blister causes extension of the lesion into adjacent normal skin. Laboratory studies show leukocytosis with eosinophilia.

options.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome with toxic epidermal necrolysis overlap
options.explanation: Correct. Drug-induced (phenytoin), ill-defined atypical targets, extensive mucosal involvement (ocular, oral, genital), positive Nikolsky sign, and 10-30% BSA involvement defines SJS-TEN overlap.

options.text: Erythema multiforme major
options.explanation: Incorrect. EM major typically follows HSV/infections, has classic three-zone targets, limited mucosal involvement, negative Nikolsky sign, and acral distribution.

options.text: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms
options.explanation: Incorrect. While drug-induced with eosinophilia, DRESS lacks the extensive mucosal involvement, positive Nikolsky sign, and characteristic skin detachment seen here.

options.text: Pemphigus vulgaris with secondary infection
options.explanation: Incorrect. Pemphigus has positive Nikolsky sign but presents with flaccid blisters, different distribution, and lacks the drug temporal relationship and extensive mucosal pattern.

options.text: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
options.explanation: Incorrect. SSSS occurs primarily in children/immunocompromised, spares mucous membranes, and lacks the drug association and target-like lesions described.

sjs-ten.explanation: This patient has SJS-TEN overlap syndrome based on several key features: drug causation (phenytoin), ill-defined atypical target lesions, extensive mucosal involvement at multiple sites (ocular, oral, genital), positive Nikolsky sign, and 10-30% BSA involvement. The temporal relationship with phenytoin initiation, the pattern of mucosal involvement, and the positive Nikolsky sign distinguish this from erythema multiforme major, which typically follows HSV infection, has classic three-zone targets, limited mucosal involvement, and negative Nikolsky sign.
sjs-ten.key_concept: Distinguishing SJS-TEN from erythema multiforme major based on etiology, target morphology, mucosal involvement, and Nikolsky sign
sjs-ten.reasoning: This question requires analysis of clinical features to distinguish SJS-TEN overlap from other severe cutaneous adverse reactions. The patient presents with drug-induced (phenytoin) severe skin reaction with characteristic features including mucosal involvement, positive Nikolsky sign, and specific BSA involvement. This represents competent-level medical knowledge requiring synthesis of multiple clinical findings to make the correct diagnosis among similar-appearing conditions.
sjs-ten.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy

sjs-ten.stem: A 45-year-old Thai man with newly diagnosed bipolar disorder presents to the emergency department 3 weeks after starting carbamazepine with fever, malaise, and a painful rash. Physical examination reveals temperature 38.9°C, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, heart rate 105/min, and respirations 20/min. There are erythematous macules with central necrosis covering 25% of his body surface area, involving his face, trunk, and extremities. Mucositis affects his oral cavity, conjunctiva, and genital area. Gentle lateral pressure on apparently normal skin results in epidermal separation. Laboratory studies show leukocytosis with eosinophilia and elevated liver enzymes. A skin biopsy demonstrates full-thickness epidermal necrosis with minimal inflammatory infiltrate.

options.text: Discontinue carbamazepine immediately and initiate supportive care in a burn unit
options.explanation: This is correct. The patient has Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN) overlap syndrome based on 25% BSA involvement, positive Nikolsky sign, and full-thickness epidermal necrosis. Thai patients have high risk for carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN due to HLA-B*15:02 association. Immediate drug discontinuation and burn unit care are essential.

options.text: Continue carbamazepine and start high-dose corticosteroids for drug hypersensitivity syndrome
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Continuing the offending drug would worsen SJS/TEN. While DRESS syndrome can present with fever and eosinophilia, the extensive mucosal involvement, positive Nikolsky sign, and full-thickness necrosis are characteristic of SJS/TEN, not DRESS.

options.text: Reduce carbamazepine dose and add antihistamines for suspected IgE-mediated drug allergy
options.explanation: This is incorrect. SJS/TEN is a T-cell mediated delayed hypersensitivity reaction, not IgE-mediated. The primary cytotoxin is granulysin from CD8+ T cells and NK cells, not histamine. Dose reduction would not prevent progression of this potentially fatal condition.

options.text: Switch to valproic acid and treat with acyclovir for suspected herpes simplex virus reactivation
options.explanation: This is incorrect. While HSV can trigger erythema multiforme, the clinical presentation with extensive BSA involvement, positive Nikolsky sign, and drug temporal relationship indicates SJS/TEN, not HSV-associated EM. The offending drug must be discontinued completely.

options.text: Obtain HLA-B*57:01 testing and start intravenous immunoglobulin while continuing current therapy
options.explanation: This is incorrect. HLA-B*57:01 is associated with abacavir hypersensitivity, not carbamazepine. The relevant association is HLA-B*15:02 in Southeast Asians for carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN. Continuing carbamazepine would be dangerous, and IVIG evidence is limited for SJS/TEN treatment.

sjs-ten.explanation: This patient presents with SJS/TEN overlap syndrome (25% BSA involvement) caused by carbamazepine. Thai patients have a strong genetic predisposition to carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN due to HLA-B*15:02 association, with up to 100-fold increased risk. Key diagnostic features include: positive Nikolsky sign (epidermal separation with lateral pressure), extensive mucosal involvement, and full-thickness epidermal necrosis on biopsy. SJS/TEN is mediated by cytotoxic T lymphocytes and NK cells releasing granulysin, causing keratinocyte apoptosis. Immediate drug discontinuation and burn unit supportive care are critical, as TEN has 20-30% mortality. The other options are incorrect because they either continue the offending drug, misidentify the mechanism (IgE vs T-cell mediated), confuse the condition with DRESS or EM, or reference wrong HLA associations.
sjs-ten.key_concept: HLA-B*15:02 association with carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN in Southeast Asian populations
sjs-ten.reasoning: This complex case requires analysis of severe cutaneous adverse drug reactions (SJS/TEN), understanding of HLA genetic associations with drug hypersensitivity in specific populations, differentiation from other drug reaction syndromes, and knowledge of appropriate emergency management. The proficient level is appropriate as it involves managing a complex, potentially fatal drug reaction with population-specific genetic risk factors.
sjs-ten.source: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Drug Allergy. NIH/NIAID. Available: niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/drug-allergy
